title: #eng-runtime: 5832109999
preview:
Priya Nair: quick sync — tradeoffs between per-tenant private queues vs a single shared backlog for continuous batching. I sketched two proposals and want quick reactions: 1) explicit per-tenant queue objects with quotas/headroom; 2) shared backlog with budget hints to admission. thoughts?

Ethan Cole: +1 to evaluating budget hints. Per-tenant queues feel simpler for isolation but explode resource tracking.

Samir Gupta: my worry with per-tenant queues is long-tail memory blowup when many idle tenants have pinned structures. We saw this in loadgen. Shared backlog + soft budgets gives better headroom packing.

Marta Lopez: can we combine? ephemeral per-tenant handles that are created on demand and retired after idle timeout. keep a compact index into shared backlog.

Ava Chen: agreement on ephemeral handles — cheap metadata, ownership lease. also need clear interface between admission and coalescer. admission should return a small opaque token, not raw queue pointer.

Priya Nair: here's the budget-hint API I prototyped:
```json
{
  "budget_hint": {
    "latency_target_ms": 50,
    "cost_priority": "low|medium|high",
    "max_tokens_estimate": 128
  }
}
```
Admission uses it for placement/credits; coalescer can use only latency_target_ms when shaping windows.

Ethan Cole: nice. we should also expose a boolean `prefetch_ok` for i/o-heavy prompts so admission can favor warm kvcache lanes.

Samir Gupta: semantics question: are budget hints soft or authoritative? i.e., do we treat them as hints the scheduler may violate under pressure? I prefer soft hints with monitoring and regression alerts.

Marta Lopez: +1 soft. if we ever enforce hard budgets we'd need billing hooks. soft hints allow graceful degradation policies: spill to dedicated pool, lower quant, or fallback model.

Ava Chen: about the interface boundary: admission -> returns AdmissionTicket {lease_id, credits, estimated_window_ms}. coalescer consumes AdmissionTicket but should never mutate scheduler state. ticket revocation via heartbeat only. avoids tight coupling.

Priya Nair: good. That implies a small ticket lifecycle: claim -> coalesce -> execute -> release. We need APIs for early release and forced revoke on overload.

Ethan Cole: observability: emit token-level traces with budget_hint and ticket lifecycle events. want to be able to reconstruct why a request got a tiny vs big window.

Samir Gupta: on long-term refactor: split admission into two phases: fast-path cheap heuristics (latency SLOs) and slow-path optimizer (packing for throughput). both share same metadata store but different time budgets. This helps keeping p99 low.

Marta Lopez: implementation note: keep admission idempotent for retries. multi-step admission must be safe under duplicate requests and client-side retries.

Ava Chen: experiments to run: 1) shared backlog + ephemeral handles with budget hints; 2) per-tenant queues with aggressive retirement; measure p50/p95/p99 and memory tail. run in canary with synthetic tenant fanout.

Priya Nair: action items: @Ethan create experiment harness (1 week), @Samir add budget_hint fields to request proto and wire up telemetry, @Marta prototype ephemeral handle lifecycle, @Ava draft ticket lifecycle API. I'll coordinate rollout gating and regression alerts.

deploy-bot: canary cluster deploy queued: batcher-v2-experiment -> staging. ETA 14m. :rocket:

Ethan Cole: noted, will start harness and share slack with metrics link.

Samir Gupta: starting proto PR today, will tag the team.

Marta Lopez: will prototype retire worker by Friday.

Ava Chen: drafting API now, will push RFC doc to /eng-runtime.

Priya Nair: thanks all — keeping this tight. if we find simple wins (budget_hint + ticket) we may avoid huge refactor. if not, split admission phases is our fallback.

title: #eng-runtime: 4722223333
preview:
Ava Chen: Hey, noticed latency spike on seqlen=128 in canary after the new kernel-autotune changes. p50 jumped ~30ms and tail worse.
Rajat Singh: Ugh. Seen that too on the nvsm108 pool. Any error logs? perf-bot, did rollout notify?
perf-bot: [deploy] rollout/arch-autotune-2026-03-15 -> 5/5 pods updated :white_check_mark:
Maya Lopez: I pulled a flamegraph from the canary, looks like we lost our KV-cache fastpath for a subset of requests. Kernel selector fell back to the generic matmul path.
Jordan Kim: Do we have the device capability signature for those nodes? SM detection might be misreporting tensorCore BF16 support.
Ava Chen: Yep. Dump from runtime probe attached below.
```
DEVICE 0: sm=8.10, tc=available, bf16=available, fp8=unknown
runtime: selected kernel path=matmul_generic
kvcache: fastpath=false
```
Rajat Singh: fp8=unknown is suspicious. The selector will disable fp8 kernels if it can't confirm support. Could be the driver returning an incomplete caps report after the kernel change.
Maya Lopez: Quick repro: run batch=8, seqlen=128 on model x-small and watch kvcache evictions + chosen kernel. I can share a reproducer script.
Jordan Kim: Please run `./tools/probe-capabilities --device 0 --verbose` and paste output. Also collect `/var/log/runtime/kernelselect.log` lines around the request.
perf-bot: Perf regression alert created: PERF-4621
Ava Chen: Ran the probe, here's the log snippet:
```
[2026-03-15T12:03:01Z] probe: hw_sig=0x5f, sm=86 detected via pci
[2026-03-15T12:03:01Z] probe: tensorcores=true bf16=true fp8_probe_timeout
[2026-03-15T12:03:02Z] kernelselect: probe returned partial caps -> conservative route
```
Maya Lopez: Looks like a timeout during fp8 microprobe, maybe due to the new prefetch optimization starving the probe thread.
Rajat Singh: Temporary mitigation: add a config gating `probe.fp8_timeout_ms=3000` -> increase timeout so probe can finish, or short-term force-enable fp8 on nodes we trust.
Jordan Kim: We should be careful force-enabling. Patch idea: if probe returns partial caps but sm >= 8.6 and tc/bf16 true, allow fp8 trial with fallback to generic kernel on error.
Ava Chen: Agreed. I can put up a small canary: allow fp8 trial for 10% of traffic with aggressive fallback and KV cache warmup disabled. Rollback option ready.
Maya Lopez: Also need a perf test harness: seqlen 32/64/128/256 microbench with batching 1/4/8 to validate kernel picks and KV hit rates.
Rajat Singh: I'll add autotune metric tags to trace: selected_kernel, kv_fastpath, probe_status, device_sig. That will help query regressions.
perf-bot: Related issue linked PERF-4621 -> PR #7892
Jordan Kim: Plan: (1) repro & gather logs (Maya/Ava), (2) small canary fp8 trial (Ava), (3) autotune trace tags (Rajat), (4) merge if stable (everyone). Target: today EOD.
Ava Chen: On it. Creating canary now :rocket:
Maya Lopez: I'll run microbench and upload flamegraphs to the thread.
Rajat Singh: I'll prepare the rollback patch just in case. :thumbsup:
Jordan Kim: Thanks all — keep updates here.

title: #eng-runtime: 3912345678
preview:
Ava Chen: Heads up — noticed a ~3x TTFT spike on /v1/chat around 10:20 UTC after the kernel-selector canary rollout. dashboard: https://console.redwood/team/rt/perf?panel=token_waterfall
Devon Patel: Saw it too. Decode time looks steady, but kernel_time attribution is much higher. Can you drop a token-level waterfall/probe?
Ava Chen: Quick probe dump pasted below:
```
route: /v1/chat  model: redwood-7b  seq_len: 128
avg_ttft: 240ms  (baseline ~80ms)
avg_decode_time: 60ms
avg_kernel_exec: 120ms
avg_kernel_queue: 40ms
kvcache_hit_rate: 0.82
kvcache_fetch_ms (p95): 90ms
selector_branch: microkernel-fallthrough (unexpected)
```
Ravi Singh: That selector_branch stands out — last selector patch added a fallthrough to microkernel for small seqs, but route seq distribution includes many 100+ prompts. Might be mis-evaluating effective seq_len due to tokenization differences.
Sasha Martín: Also seeing microbatch size distribution skew: many single-request batches after the scheduler tweak. Small batches raise per-token scheduling overhead.
observability-bot: automated probe summary: token_latency_breakdown (per-route /v1/chat): prequeue=30ms, kernel_queue=40ms, kernel_exec=120ms, postproc=50ms -> ttft=240ms
Devon Patel: kernel_exec 120ms is abnormally high. Question: is kernel_exec inflated because we include kvcache remote fetch time inside kernel time? We should separate kvcache_fetch_ms from kernel_exec in the waterfall.
Ava Chen: I traced one sample: kernel started, tried local shard, missed -> triggered remote shard fetch (89ms) then resumed kernel exec. So remote kvcache fetch is being counted inside kernel_exec in current instrumentation. snippet:
```
[2026-03-18T10:19:45Z] kernel_start token_idx=12
[2026-03-18T10:19:45Z] kvcache_get shard0 -> MISS
[2026-03-18T10:19:45Z] kvcache_fetch_remote(shard0) 89ms
[2026-03-18T10:19:46Z] kernel_exec resumed, exec_ms=30ms
```
Ravi Singh: Makes sense. Actionable items: (1) add explicit kvcache_fetch_ms field to token waterfall and metric labels, (2) add selector_branch tag to kernel metrics so we can attribute exec/queue by branch, (3) instrument microbatch_size in the probe to correlate small batches with TTFT.
Sasha Martín: I'll open a dashboard PR to add panels: kvcache_fetch_ms heatmap, selector_branch pie, microbatch_size histogram, and an alert: TTFT > 200ms sustained for 5m on /v1/chat.
Ava Chen: I'm going to rollback the selector canary for now and re-run the sampler. Pinging @infra-bot to start rollback. :pray:
infra-bot: rollback initiated for canary pool id=canary-3. Estimated completion: ~4m. :gear:
Devon Patel: After rollback completes, re-run token-sampler with the same probe config and post diffs (ttft/decode/kernel/kvcache). If TTFT returns to baseline we'll revert the change and do offline selector thresholds tuning.
Ava Chen: Agreed. I'll post results in ~10m. Thanks all. :white_check_mark:

title: #eng-runtime: 3419876543
preview:
Priya Patel: Heads-up — after the kv-prefetch tweak landed in canary, we're seeing 2-3x p95 for short sequences (<32 tokens) on nv8p. Latency cliff around 100ms -> 300ms for short-paths. I put a few request traces and flamegraphs in the perf gist. Anyone seen kernel/kvm interactions with prefetch?

Diego Morales: Yep saw the spike on dashboards. Micro-profiling shows an extra memcpy on the kernel selection hot path when prefetch engages. Batcher tends to coalesce a few short requests into a minislab which then picks a fused-kernel that has higher setup cost.

Ava Chen: Kernel chooser currently looks at seq-length histogram + kv_hit_estimate to pick fused vs fastpath. Prefetch warms KV and increments the "active tokens" accounting, which looks like longer sequences to the selector — could be double-counting KV warmup in the heuristic.

Marta Ivanov: Quick mitigation idea: add a "short-lane reserve" in the queue steward so short sequences get a small reserved token budget and avoid being coalesced with long ones. Also tune prefetch depth for short lanes. Draft config snippet below: 

```
queue_steward:
  short_lane:
    reserve_tokens_per_sec: 150
    max_coalesce_size: 4
    prefetch_depth: 1   # shallow prefetch for short-path
  long_lane:
    dynamic_fair_share: true
```

Priya Patel: Would that reserve reduce overall throughput for long tenants? Concern about unfairness / eviction overhead.

Diego Morales: We can make it proportional: short-lane gets a soft reserve (not hard cap) and we replenish from long-lane if idle. Also add a hedging factor so reserve scales by occupancy. On kernel side we can add a hint: if batch composition >= 80% short sequences prefer low-setup kernels.

Ava Chen: Agree. Add a kernel_hint label on batches (kernel_hint: prefer_low_setup) so selection avoids heavy fused kernels when prefetch/kv warmups are the dominant cost. Also record the reason in traces: admit_reason = "short_reserve" or "coalesced". That'll make A/B analysis easier.

infra-bot: deploy: canary/prefetch-harmony-v2 -> prod-rollout started by @ci (links: https://ci.redwood/rollouts/1234).

Priya Patel: Experiment plan: A/B 20% nodes for 24h, collect p50/p95/p99 by seq-length bucket (0-8,9-32,33-128) and kv_hit_rate. Add extra metrics: kernel_hint, admit_reason. I pushed a short smoke test: /infra/perf/smoke/short_prefetch_probe.sh

Diego Morales: I'll run the mini-bench and attach flamegraphs. For fast mitigation today we can set prefetch_depth=1 for short-path and reduce prefetch concurrency from 8->2. That should cut warmup memcpy contention.

Marta Ivanov: Ack. I'll prepare a rollback job and a small feature-flag flip so we can toggle short-lane reserve if the canary regresses. :thumbsup:

Priya Patel: Thanks all — prioritizing this for morning triage. Will post consolidated perf artifacts and a short checklist. :coffee:

title: #eng-runtime: 2365550000
preview:
Jordan Blake: Starting thread to finalize PRD scope for kernel pluggability + KV policy split. Want minimal launch gates and clear ownership.
Priya Rao: Can we commit to splitting into two releases? KV defaults first, kernel selector second. Product needs conservative defaults for tenants.
Marta Gomez: Agreed. KV rollout items: per-tenant namespace ON by default, memory budget per-tenant, and tenant opt-out API. Add lifecycle key rotation on tenant delete.
Evan Li: For kernel selector, we need a compatibility matrix test and a graceful fallback path. Suggest runtime flag `--kernel-mode=stable|exp` and per-route annotation for opt-in.
Sasha Levin: Acceptance draft: 
- KV: default TTL 30m, LRU eviction, per-tenant memory guardrails, no cross-tenant reads.
- Kernel selector v0: support 2 kernel families, P95 latency <= +10% vs baseline for buckets [8,64,512], generation QA delta < 1.0% on core eval.
Jordan Blake: Ops rollout: canary progression 0.5% -> 3% -> 15% with 24h soak at 15% for KV; kernel selector gets 0.5% -> 5% -> 25% with 48h soak at 25% for high-risk models. Automated rollback if error >2x baseline for 15m.
Priya Rao: Observability must include kernel_selection.trace_id, kernel_version metric, and audit events for config changes. Product gating: audit logs + rollback <2m required for enterprise sign-off.
Marta Gomez: Edge cases to capture in RFC: mixed-batch bucketization by kernel capability, long-context eviction backstop for >2048 tokens, and custom CUDA op detection leading to auto-fallback.
Evan Li: CI/Perf: baseline harness = prod snapshot from 2026-02-08 run scripts. Perf jobs must publish raw histograms and per-bucket diffs. Add integration test that runs KV+selector together in smaller infra.
Sasha Levin: Security: namespace encryption at rest, key rotation policy, and tenant namespace deletion workflow.
InfraBot: deploy:canary created id=cp-2365550000, clusters=[us-east-1-canary], target=0.5%
Jordan Blake: Action items: Jordan update PRD + GH PR; Priya draft KV rollout playbook; Marta add compat matrix and tests; Evan add CI perf jobs; Sasha write observability + security checklist. Review on GH by Fri EOD.

Jordan Blake: Ops rollout: canary progression 0.5% -> 3% -> 15% with 24h soak at 15% for KV; kernel selector gets 0.5% -> 5% -> 25% with 48h soak at 25% for high-risk models. Automated rollback if error >2x baseline for 15m.
Priya Rao: Observability must include kernel_selection.trace_id, kernel_version metric, and audit events for config changes. Product gating: audit logs + rollback <2m required for enterprise sign-off.
Marta Gomez: Edge cases to capture in RFC: mixed-batch bucketization by kernel capability, long-context eviction backstop for >2048 tokens, and custom CUDA op detection leading to auto-fallback.
Evan Li: CI/Perf: baseline harness = prod snapshot from 2026-02-08 run scripts. Perf jobs must publish raw histograms and per-bucket diffs. Add integration test that runs KV+selector together in smaller infra.
Sasha Levin: Security: namespace encryption at rest, key rotation policy, and tenant namespace deletion workflow.
InfraBot: deploy:canary created id=cp-2365550000, clusters=[us-east-1-canary], target=0.5%
Jordan Blake: Action items: Jordan update PRD + GH PR; Priya draft KV rollout playbook; Marta add compat matrix and tests; Evan add CI perf jobs; Sasha write observability + security checklist. Review on GH by Fri EOD.

title: #eng-runtime: 2248903333
preview:
Ava Chen: OOM spike observed on gpu-pool-3 just now. allocator failed to reserve workspace for batch=8, seq_len=2048 during a KV warmup surge. stack excerpt attached below.

Joel Park: I saw the same. kernel selector jumped to a full-fprop matmul path and memory jumped ~1.8GB. batching heuristic allowed batch growth during warmup which made it worse.

Sana R.: Can we do a quick failover: immediate circuit-breaker -> route to quantized-variant + reduce max_batch from 8 to 4 for that pool? That should avoid large workspace allocations.

Joel Park: +1. Also propose a short-term allocator tweak: prefer workspace reuse over fresh reserve when free_mem_pct < 25%. simple config: 
```
allocator: {
  prefer_reuse_threshold_pct: 25,
  max_new_reserve_mb: 512
}
```
This drops 90% of the transient OOMs in my local repro.

Mike L.: Add metric/alert: `allocator.reserve_fail_rate` and `kvcache.miss_burst` > 50 over 1m. That would trigger circuit-breaker before user-impact.

deploy-bot: route update: `pool-3` -> `quant-v2` canary applied (60s ago). rolling rule reduces `max_batch` to 4 for matches with seq_len > 1024.

Ava Chen: Nice. Can we also add a temporary request-shaping rule: drop/trim requests with seq_len > 4096 and send 413 with suggestion header? Better to fail fast than cascade OOM.

Sana R.: I'll add the request-shaper config. snippet: 
```
request_shaper: {
  max_seq_len: 4096,
  fallback_policy: { type: "reject_with_hint", hint: "try_quantized_or_smaller_seq" }
}
```

Joel Park: I'll open PR for the allocator tweak + dynamic_batch_cap heuristic (cap = floor(free_mem_mb / per_token_ws_mb / 2)). Will include unit tests and perf microbenchmark.

Mike L.: I'll wire up the dashboard panels for `allocator.reserve_fail_rate`, `free_mem_percentile_10m`, and `kvcache.eviction_rate`. Also create alert playbook linking this thread.

Ava Chen: Thanks. Quick rollback plan: if canary increases tail latency > 100ms or error rate > 0.5% in 5m, rollback route to previous variant and restore max_batch=8.

Sana R.: Scheduling canary for 20:00 PST today and will monitor.

Joel Park: PR coming in ~30m.

Ava Chen: Action items: Joel -> PR for allocator + batching heuristic; Sana -> request-shaper + canary ops; Mike -> metrics + alert; ops -> update runbook with circuit-breaker steps. :thumbsup:

title: #eng-runtime: 2178004321
preview:
Ava Chen: heads up — around 09:42 UTC we saw p99 jump from ~220ms to ~1.3s on a handful of requests. correlates with a KV cache eviction spike when concurrency hit ~620 on g4-prod pools. not a full outage but SLOs are unhappy. anyone on call?

Miguel Rojas: looking. do we have a flamegraph / profile from the time window? also which kernel was active when the tail spiked?

Riya Patel: sharing quick profile + logs. flamegraph (sample): https://perf.redwood/internal/flame/2178004321.html — shows heavy time in `kv_lookup` + `kv_evict` and then long stalls in `kernel_dispatch` due to fallback to generic matmul path.

Riya Patel: short log excerpt:
```
[09:42:13] kv: miss -> evict key=usr-embedding-xxxx
[09:42:13] kv: evict start (heap=0.83)
[09:42:13] runtime: switch kernel=opt_gemm -> fallback_generic (seq=512)
[09:42:13] request: wait for kv-lock 12ms
[09:42:14] request: kernel fallback + microbatch stall 480ms
```

perf-bot: :warning: perf-alert: p99_latency > 1s for route `/v1/generate` (prod-us-west) — correlated: KV eviction events increased by 4.1x in 1m window.

Kenji Sato: quick hypothesis: evictions causing KV miss storm which inflates seq lengths hitting kernels that don't have optimized kernels (or wrong kernel chosen due to cache miss timing). microbatch gating currently uses fixed `max_mb_per_worker=12`. at high concurrency that leads to more per-request cache churn.

Ava Chen: proposals? fast mitigations first please.

Riya Patel: immediate mitigation we can try (non-disruptive): raise `kv_cache_hysteresis` from 0.6 -> 0.82 and lower `max_mb_per_worker` to 8 for prod-us-west via hot-config. command: `redwoodctl set runtime.kv_cache.hysteresis 0.82 --env=prod-us-west` and `redwoodctl set runtime.batch.max_mb_per_worker 8 --env=prod-us-west`

Miguel Rojas: agree. also add short-term kernel selection tweak: prefer `opt_int8_gemm` for sequences > 256 when `kv_miss_rate > 0.15` to avoid fallback penalty. config snippet:
```yaml
runtime:
  kernel_selection:
    fallback_policy: prefer_quant_when_kv_unstable: true
    kv_miss_threshold: 0.15
```

Kenji Sato: for safety I'd canary the batch change across 10% of nodes first. we should also add a micro probe that measures p99 conditioned on kv_hit_rate buckets.

Ava Chen: can we get a quick before/after test locally? Riya can you run a replay over a 10k req window with concurrency ramp to 700?

Riya Patel: ran a replay on staging-2 with config tweaks. results (p95/p99):
```
base (no changes): p95=210ms p99=980ms
with hysteresis 0.82 + max_mb=8: p95=230ms p99=320ms
+ kernel_tweak prefer_quant: p95=225ms p99=300ms
```

Miguel Rojas: nice — major win on tail. also looked at CPU side: eviction IO thrash dropped by ~60% in the replay.

Ava Chen: ok let's do the 10% canary rollout of the batch change + kernel tweak. Riya/Kenji to own rollout, Miguel to monitor perf dashboards and revert if p99 > 600ms. I'll notify infra and support.

Kenji Sato: scheduling canary now — will post canary thread. rolling to 10% in 5m.

perf-bot: :white_check_mark: canary started (10% nodes) — watch keys: /dashboard/p95, /dashboard/p99, /alerts/p95regress

Ava Chen: thanks everyone. follow-up action items: (1) add kv-hit-rate conditioned p99 metric to per-route dashboards (Miguel), (2) explore adaptive microbatcher that lowers batch size when kv_eviction_rate rises (we'll scope next sprint, Kenji/Riya).

Riya Patel: noted. I'll document the hot-config commands and link flamegraph to the incident doc.

Miguel Rojas: I'm leaving a quick profiling run command here for the runbook: `pprof --text /tmp/prod-2178004321.pb | head -n 50`

Ava Chen: :thumbsup: good work. tagging this thread for postmortem TL;DR.

title: #eng-runtime: 2175000123
preview:
Ava Chen: Heads up — I kicked off the new bench-harness nightly run across Ampere/H100 for the candidate kernel set, full seqlen sweep 1..8192, microbatch sizes 1/2/4.
perf-bot: Run started: id=nn-2026-03-17-01, jobs=48, config=arch-aware+kv-probe
Diego Morales: Noted. Which candidate showed issues?
Ava Chen: candidate: kgen-v2-archmix-17. At seqlen=4096 we see p99 latency spike and throughput drop ~27% on H100. On Ampere it's fine. Uploading flamegraph and perf data.
Ava Chen: ```bash
# repro command
./bench --kernel kgen-v2-archmix-17 --seq 4096 --batch 1 --hw h100 --warm 50 --iter 200
```
Priya Nair: did you check KV cache path? That's where seqlen-specific code paths tend to diverge.
Ava Chen: yes, toggled KV on/off. With KV off the spike disappears. With KV on, perf trace shows extra memcpy in gather step (memcpy3 at gather_kv.c:214).
Samir Gupta: Could be alignment/packing on H100 kernels. kgen-v2 uses a vectorized gather that falls back to slow path for sequences that align a certain way. Strange pattern though.
Diego Morales: Also check batching heuristic: maybe continuous batching merges prefixes differently at seqlen boundary causing inefficient kernel selection. Which kernel was actually picked by runtime at seq=4096?
Ava Chen: Runtime picked kernel id=archmix_gather_kv_fast then fell back to archmix_gather_scalar after ~120ms. Autotuner metric shows "failed_perf=1.28x" and logged: selected=archmix_gather_kv_fast,fallback=archmix_gather_scalar
Priya Nair: Maybe autotuner mismeasured because of microbench noise. Re-run microprobe with isolation (CPU affined, no other jobs).
Ava Chen: Requeued an isolated microprobe run: mp-2175000128. Will post results.
perf-bot: mp-2175000128 completed: archmix_gather_kv_fast throughput=435 t/s, archmix_gather_scalar throughput=600 t/s at seq=4096 on H100. Noise sigma ~2.1%.
Samir Gupta: That's a big delta. Sounds like archmix_gather_kv_fast regresses on H100 for that seqlen. We should mark it flaky and block from nightly promotions.
Diego Morales: Agreed. Proposal: gating rule — if variant <0.85 of best for seq in critical set {1024,2048,4096} on a given architecture, fail candidate.
Priya Nair: Also add a KV-specific microbenchmark to the harness that exercises gather+kv heavy path across seqlen boundaries (including multiples of 256/512).
Ava Chen: I'll open PR to update bench-harness: add kv-heavy probe, tighten noise thresholds, ensure isolated nodes for H100 tests. Tentative PR: #3124.
Liam O'Connor: Also note mitigation: quick fix is to fallback to scalar gather on H100 until vectorized path is fixed. Prepare rollout plan (canary -> full).
Ava Chen: Plan: 1) block promotion for kgen-v2-archmix-17 2) runtime config: enforce fallback for H100 for seq=4096 3) debug/fix kernel 4) re-run full sweep.
Diego Morales: Any chance this is driver-related? What driver/CUDA versions on failing runs?
Ava Chen: H100 host: driver 525.85, CUDA 12.1. Ampere runs used CUDA 11.8. I'll repro with driver matrix.
Samir Gupta: If driver dependent, schedule driver-matrix sweep (525.x vs 530.x, CUDA 12.1/12.2).
perf-bot: Nightly sweep summary: candidates_evaluated=42, blocked=1 (kgen-v2-archmix-17), flaky=2, regressions_auto-blocked=1.
Priya Nair: Thanks. Ava, once PR is up and you have repro notes ping #eng-runtime and cc me.
Ava Chen: Will do. Uploading logs: https://files.redwood/test-runs/mp-2175000128.tar.gz :thumbsup:
Diego Morales: Good triage. Let's also add a short checklist in the harness README for seqlen-sensitive kernels (KV toggles, isolated runs, driver matrix).
Ava Chen: Adding checklist to PR. Closing loop when mp re-run + driver sweep finish.

title: #eng-runtime: 2122223333
preview:
Ava Chen: FYI I rolled the updated kernel-selection heuristic to a 10% canary this morning and were seeing a p95/p99 latency climb on gen-rt hosts. Anyone on call?
Ravi Patel: On it — whats the delta, numbers?
Ava Chen: Quick numbers from dashboards: before vs after on mixed workloads (1k token tails):
```
before: p95=72ms p99=72ms
after:  p95=110ms p99=210ms
hosts: gen-rt-*-pool
trace: trace_id=abc123
```
Marta Lopez: Timeline? who deployed? rollout percent?
Ava Chen: Canary at 06:12 UTC (10%). Full rollout was scheduled for 08:00 but I paused when alerts tripped.
deploy-bot: Canary rollout paused at 08:05 by @Ava Chen — rollback available: `/deploy rollback kernel-selector:v3`
Ravi Patel: Can we bisect kernel choices? I suspect an interaction between KV prefetch behavior and the fused kernel choice at batch boundaries. Can someone run a perf profile on an affected host?
Noah Kim: Running `./perf/profile --target gen-rt-12 --samples 200` now. Will paste top flame lines when done.
Noah Kim: Perf flame (top):
```
1. flash_variant_x         34%  (hot path)
2. fused_attn_dispatch     18%
3. paged_attn_kernel       16%
4. kv_prefetch_wait        12%
```
Marta Lopez: Seeing similar on 3 hosts. Batching heuristics appear to pack mixed-length requests such that the fused preference flips mid-batch; when that happens KV cache is often cold and we fall through to paged path.
Ava Chen: Hypothesis: new selector aggressively prefers fused-flash for mixed batches; on seq-length transitions the KV cache misses and we incur paged-attn tail latency. Suggest temporary config: `stable_fallback=true` to prefer fallback kernel until KV warms.
Ravi Patel: Quick test: set env `KERNEL_SELECTOR_STRICT=0` and `KERNEL_FALLBACK_THRESHOLD=0.6` on canary, re-run perf-check.
perf-bot: Perf-check job queued: job_id=perf-21222-01 target=gen-rt-canary (ETA ~8m)
Ava Chen: If perf-check shows improvement we'll either revert the selector commit or tune heuristics. For now leave canary paused.
Noah Kim: Perf job finished: p95 back to 75ms, p99=85ms. Raw: `p95=75 p99=85 samples=600` — looks promising.
Marta Lopez: Confirmed across 3 canary hosts. Root cause looks like the interaction: aggressive fused preference + tight batch boundary heuristics.
Ravi Patel: I'll open a bisect/tweak PR to lower fused preference default to 0.4 and add a guard in KV prefetch logic to avoid prefetching on boundary <=2. Tagging @eng-runtime for review.
Ava Chen: Thanks. Please add a stress integration test (mixing seq lengths) and document rollback steps in the release notes.
Noah Kim: Rolling rollback on canary to `v2-kselector` now.
deploy-bot: Rollback complete on canary at 09:12 UTC — statuses OK.
Marta Lopez: Follow-up: schedule slower rollout of the kernel selector behind a feature flag and add a perf guard: if p99 rises >25% over baseline within 1h abort rollout.
Ava Chen: :thumbsup: Good plan. Let's sync tomorrow for deeper perf triage and then re-evaluate the selector after tests.

Ava Chen: Thanks. Please add a stress integration test (mixing seq lengths) and document rollback steps in the release notes.
Noah Kim: Rolling rollback on canary to `v2-kselector` now.
deploy-bot: Rollback complete on canary at 09:12 UTC — statuses OK.
Marta Lopez: Follow-up: schedule slower rollout of the kernel selector behind a feature flag and add a perf guard: if p99 rises >25% over baseline within 1h abort rollout.
Ava Chen: :thumbsup: Good plan. Let's sync tomorrow for deeper perf triage and then re-evaluate the selector after tests.

title: #eng-runtime: 2105012345
preview:
Ava Chen: heads-up — canary runs on int8-quantized Llama-3 variant show a slow, steady memory increase on long-token sessions ( > 50k tokens). Not a sharp leak, more like a slope. Seen on two different GPU SKUs.
Sam Ortiz: can you paste the profiler slice? memory slope sounds like either KV residency not dropping or fragmentation causing free-list starvation.
Ava Chen: putting a small dump. profiler snapshot at t=75k tokens:
```
KV: total_slots=1_200_000 used_slots=742_312 free_slots=457_688
slab_frag_bytes=34_432_768 free_list_len=3
page_allocs=11245 page_evictions=52
mem_total_gb=28.9 mem_used_gb=23.6 mem_slope_mb_per_min=3.2
quant_mode=INT8 kernel=fast_attn_v3 batch_heuristic=adaptive_lowlat
```
Ava Chen: note slab_frag_bytes >> expected (~8MB baseline). mem_slope for BF16 runs is ~0.7 MB/min under same load.
Liam Patel: suspicious: fast_attn_v3 has a different kv-page alignment/padding. INT8 path seems to allocate slightly wider KV pages (padding to 256B) which can multiply fragmentation when many small entries exist.
Maya Rios: i ran a short AB test switching kernel to fast_attn_v2 + int8, slope dropped to ~1.1 MB/min and slab_frag_bytes ~9MB. Accuracy hit was tiny but reproducible: rouge-ish metrics show -0.6% after sustained long sessions.
Sam Ortiz: can you share per-request KV churn? if the batching heuristic is switching to smaller batches for low-latency, that increases KV write frequency which interacts badly with fragmentation.
Ava Chen: here's an excerpt from request-level telemetry (4min window):
```
reqs=420 avg_batch_size=3 median_seq_len=512 writes_per_min=1_200 kv_writes=18_900
avg_kv_entry_size=184 bytes hot_prefix_hit=0.23
```
Ava Chen: writes_per_min high because adaptive_lowlat flips to per-request flush for sequences > 256, leading to more frequent partial page fills.
Sam Ortiz: ok that would explain free_list_len drop. free list of 3 means allocs fall back to new pages instead of reusing freed fragments.
Maya Rios: accuracy delta note: INT8 impact is correlated with which KV pages get evicted vs paged — when a hot block with mixed-precision keys gets compacted we saw a ~0.8% top1 perplexity bump for short windows after eviction. It recovers after warmup.
Liam Patel: hypothesis: two factors compounding — (1) kernel's allocation alignment pads small entries causing fragmentation; (2) batching heuristic increasing write frequency creating many small partially-filled pages. Combined leads to gradual mem growth.
perf-bot: alert: kv_mem_slope > 2 MB/min for 30m (canary-fleet: int8-fast_attn_v3). Pager: eng-runtime on call.
Ava Chen: proposed experiments:
- run int8 + fast_attn_v2 for 24h canary (we did a short run, extend it)
- run int8 + fast_attn_v3 but force batch_heuristic=throughput (bigger batches)
- instrument allocator to expose per-slab free-list distribution and reclamation latency
- add a metric: kv_fragmentation_ratio = slab_frag_bytes / mem_used
Maya Rios: I've already started the 24h int8+v2 canary. Link to heatmap and perf dashboard: https://console.redwood.ai/kvm/heatmaps/longrun/abcd-123
Sam Ortiz: quick mitigation we can ship: conservative change to allocator to prefer coalescing small frees into a reclaimable bucket when free_list_len < 16. Also throttle per-request KV flush when avg_batch_size < 4 (to prefer coalescing).
Liam Patel: coalescing will add CPU cost — need to measure kernel selection tradeoffs. If we coalesce aggressively, int8-v3 might lose its low-latency benefit. We should quantify: added CPU ~+6% for coalescing loop.
Ava Chen: actionables agreed:
1) extend Maya's 24h run (Maya)
2) add allocator telemetry and kv_fragmentation_ratio (Sam)
3) run int8+v3 with throughput batching for 8h to see slope change (Ava)
4) prototype coalescing patch behind a feature flag and measure latency delta (Liam)
5) open tracking issue with perf trace and sample dumps (Ava -> will create)
Sam Ortiz: also flag for eval team: add a regression set that includes long-session prompts to catch post-warmup accuracy drift (Maya can coordinate)
Maya Rios: noted, i'll add 3 prompt tracks: synthetic long chat, real customer long-draft, and iterative summarization.
Ava Chen: FYI, here's the repro command I used for the short run:
```
./canary_runner --model llama3-int8 --kernel fast_attn_v3 --batch-heuristic adaptive_lowlat --session-length 100000 --emit-telemetry
```
Liam Patel: one more insight from local sim: if we force page_size=4k vs 16k padding, frag_bytes halves. Kernel's buffer alignment macro was rounding up to 16k for int8 path for vectorization reasons.
Sam Ortiz: if that alignment is the culprit we can try smaller vector width kernels or add a per-arch override. But that's deeper change — try allocator mitigations first.
perf-bot: note: created incident-xxx with logs and the initial profiler snapshot.
Ava Chen: thanks all — i'll file the issue and paste links. keeping this thread as the single source for the canary observations and action list. :thumbsup:

title: #eng-runtime: 2071112222
preview:
Priya Rao: heads up — saw three multi-gpu NCCL jobs hang in us-west-2b ~00:14 UTC after the TOR config push. jobs were pinned to nodes in rack r12.
Alex Chen: confirmed, started getting alerts from autoscaler + job-controller. affected runs are all 8xA100 p2-style instances. only east-west traffic, intra-pod.
Mateo Alvarez: grabbing logs now. looks like peer QP timeouts followed by qp state stuck in ERROR. attaching quick snippets.
Mateo Alvarez: ```
[ 10.123456] mlx5_core 0000:86:00.0: QP 0x12a3a: fatal error: access violation (retry exceeded)
[ 10.123457] ib_qp_event: QP 0x12a3a reported error state
[ 10.123458] ibv_post_send failed: Invalid argument (22)
```
Jing Wu: did we roll any kernel or driver updates around midnight?
Priya Rao: yes — TOR team pushed a vxlan-offload and TSO/GSO tuning change plus a small bonding driver tweak at 00:05. they said it was a non-disruptive config-only change.
OpsKevin: @PriyaRao can you paste the TOR change? I saw they flipped vxlan offload to 'on' for performance; previous default was off.
Priya Rao: TOR change snippet: ```
etdev: set vxlan_hw_launch=1
nethtool -K vxlan0 hw-tc-offload on
```
Alex Chen: interesting — we usually see issues when HW offloads interact with mlx5 checksum/segmentation offloads. RDMA relies on consistent MTU and non-modified packets for RoCEv2.
Claire Summers: quick repro plan: cordon two nodes in r12, run ib_send_bw and ibv_rc_pingpong to see if raw RDMA flows fail independent of NCCL. Mateo, can you run that?
Mateo Alvarez: ran ibv_rc_pingpong between gpu nodes 12-03 and 12-04. results:
Mateo Alvarez: ```
# ibv_rc_pingpong -d mlx5_0 10.0.12.3 10.0.12.4
Can't get completion event: -110
Qpair stalled after 2s
```
Jing Wu: did you check ethtool -k on the vxlan and phys iface?
Mateo Alvarez: ethtool -k shows TSO/GSO on for eth0 and vxlan0, rx-checksumming on. phys MTU is 9000 but vxlan MTU collapsed to 1500 after the TOR offload change on some paths.
Priya Rao: that MTU mismatch smells like PMTU blackhole with offloads rewriting packets. RoCEv2 uses UDP; fragmentation/encap can break RDMA headers.
SREbot: incident created: INC-2071 — "RoCEv2 qpair stalls after TOR vxlan offload change" and paging on-call.
OpsKevin: temporary mitigation: disable vxlan hw-offload on TORs or force vxlan mtu to 9000 so encapsulated path preserves headroom. Which is faster?
Claire Summers: fastest is to flip back TOR vxlan-offload to previous state — roll back and watch. If not possible quickly, we can disable TSO/GSO on host veth/vxlan interfaces as a host-side band-aid.
Alex Chen: host commands to try on affected nodes:```
# disable offloads on vxlan interface
ethtool -K vxlan0 gso off gro off tso off txvlan off rxvlan off
# ensure vxlan mtu
ip link set dev vxlan0 mtu 9000
```
Mateo Alvarez: applied host mitigations on two nodes (disabled gso/gro/tso on vxlan0). re-ran ibv_rc_pingpong and ib_send_bw: now clean, no qpair stalls.
Mateo Alvarez: ```
# ibv_rc_pingpong -d mlx5_0 10.0.12.3 10.0.12.4
bytes 1: 4096  4096  1.0 usec avg
```
Priya Rao: can we get TORs rolled back? OpsKevin coordinating with TOR team now.
OpsKevin: TORs rolled back to previous vxlan offload setting at 00:42 UTC for r12. Monitoring for 10m.
Jing Wu: follow-ups: add a test in our preflight that exercises RoCEv2 path after any TOR offload change; add a guard that rejects vxlan mtu < 9000 for GPU racks.
Claire Summers: also add a runbook step: if RDMA qpair stalls show up, check vxlan/phys MTU and host offloads (gro/gso/tso).
Priya Rao: summary: root cause = vxlan hw-offload + TOR cfg change causing vxlan MTU/segmentation mismatch leading to encapsulation fragmentation and RDMA QP stalls. host-side mitigation (disable GSO/GRO/TSO on vxlan) restored connectivity; TOR rollback permanent fix.
Alex Chen: filing a small RFC to add automated validation for RDMA-sensitive offload changes. cc: @networking @eng-infra
SREbot: INC-2071 status -> resolved (mitigation applied + TOR rollback).

title: #eng-runtime: 2003456789
preview:
Priya Patel: Heads up — our embedding websocket blackbox in asia-south1 started throwing transient 5xx + handshake timeouts around 03:10 UTC. SLO alert for p95 latency fired. Anyone seeing cluster/backplane signals?

probe-bot: Alert: probe/emb-ws-asia-south1 increased error_rate > 3% over 5m. Probe rate=1/s. Sample payload size=512 tokens.

Mateo Alvarez: I saw a small spike in pod restarts on the gpu-pool in that region at the same time. CPU on the sidecars ~80% for short bursts.

Liu Chen: Noticing a lot of short-lived SNAT allocations in the NAT gateway metrics — ephemeral port reuse went up. Could be ephemeral port exhaustion causing handshake failures (we saw this pattern before).

Ava Kim: Is the synthetic probe sampling rate the same across all regions? Cost-wise we run 1/s in asia-south1 because it's a high-traffic sim, but that might be overkill during low-traffic hours.

Priya Patel: Yes it's currently 1/s. This thread is mainly to decide if it's a noisy SLO (probe-induced) vs real capacity issue. Two asks: 1) correlate probe errors with actual user traffic for embeddings; 2) tune probes/alerts so they don't create false positives and cost blowups.

Mateo Alvarez: Quick correlate: user embeddings QPS during the spike was flat (no user-side increase). So more likely probe noise or infra-wide transient.

Liu Chen: Proposed immediate mitigations: reduce probe rate to 1/10s for asia-south1 during the 00-08 UTC window, enable connection pooling on the probe runner side, and increase the prometheus alert window from 5m to 10m to reduce flapping alerts.

Ava Kim: Agree on rate reduction. We should also adjust the alert rule to use a rolling quantile/histogram to avoid single-bucket bursts triggering SLOs.

Priya Patel: Can someone propose concrete prometheus rule/Dashboard changes? I want minimal risk and clear rollback.

Liu Chen: Draft rule snippet (quick):

```
# use avg over 10m and require sustained error ratio
- alert: EmbeddingsProbeHighErrorSustained
  expr: rate(probe_errors_total{job="emb-ws", region="asia-south1"}[10m]) / rate(probe_requests_total{job="emb-ws", region="asia-south1"}[10m]) > 0.02
  for: 10m
  labels:
    severity: page
  annotations:
    summary: "Sustained embed probe errors in asia-south1"
```

Mateo Alvarez: Also add `ignore_common_noise` label for bot-originated probes so dashboards don't aggregate them with user-facing checks.

ops-bot: Note: adjusting probe rate will reduce synthetic billing; estimate ~65% saving in asia-south1 probe spend for the night window.

Priya Patel: Action items: Liu to roll the prom rule + change scrape/alert windows (canary in staging), Mateo to throttle probe runners (change from 1/s -> 0.1/s) for the quiet hours and enable pooling, Ava to update Grafana panels to use histogram_quantile(0.95, ...) and add a "synthetic-only" series. I'll monitor the alerts for 6 hours and confirm.

Ava Kim: Quick ops note — for dashboard smoothing use `increase()` on counters with `offset` windows for noise immunity. I'll add a small panel description explaining why thresholds are higher for Asia.

Mateo Alvarez: Rolling the probe change now to a 10% sample and enabling keepalive pooling. Command I used on the probe-runner k8s job: 

```
kubectl -n infra set env job/probe-runner SAMPLE_RATE=0.1 KEEPALIVE=true && kubectl rollout restart job/probe-runner -n infra
```

Liu Chen: I've created a PR for the alert rule with the `for: 10m` and region scoping. Linking in case reviewers want to tweak: https://github.com/redwood-inference/infra-monitoring/pull/482

Priya Patel: Thanks team. Reopening if this recurs after the changes. If we still see errors with reduced synthetic load, escalate to infra-networking + cloud provider for NAT scaling.

ops-bot: Reminder: cost impact estimate saved to ticket INF-2784.

Ava Kim: Will add a small note to the SLO runbook about probe sampling and cost/false-positive tradeoffs. :white_check_mark: 

Priya Patel: Closing for now — will follow up with a short postmortem if we need deeper action. :thumbsup:

title: #eng-runtime: 1967890123
preview:
Alex Chen: Heads up — seeing a TTFT spike on mixed int4+fp8 runs for long-prefill workloads on rtx-pool. Numbers from yesterday's smoke are attached below, looks like the dequant hotpath is getting invoked more during prefill for long contexts.

Priya Rao: Ugh. Is this the prefill-only profile or end-to-end? Any chance kv-eviction frequency changed?

Alex Chen: Prefill-heavy. End-to-end shows TTFT jump even though TPS is stable. KV eviction metrics are normal. Sending the pprof slice.

Mateo Alvarez: paste profiling snippet:
```
# bench: longprefill_mixcalib (1000 tokens)
prefill_total_ms: 3620
  - matmul_kernels_ms: 2100
  - kv_dequant_ms: 840
  - kv_copy_ms: 180
decode_total_ms: 1850
  - matmul_kernels_ms: 1200
  - kv_dequant_ms: 120
  - overhead_ms: 530
tokens: 1000
TTFT_ms_per_token_prefill: 3.62
TTFT_ms_per_token_decode: 1.85
TPS_prefill: 276
TPS_decode: 540
# baseline (no mixed calib): prefill 2.1ms/token, decode 1.9ms/token
```

Jun Park: Interesting — baseline had prefill faster than decode, now prefill is slower. That 840ms kv_dequant is suspicious. Might be dequant happening eagerly during prefill because of calibration fallback.

Grace Liu: Could this be the new kernel-selection heuristic picking fp8 matmul + int4 kv dequant path for certain layers? We changed the score func last week to prefer lower memory.

Alex Chen: Exactly my thought. I toggled kernel-selection to prefer stable int8 kernels for layers > 32 and re-ran: prefill dropped to 2.05ms/token, decode ~1.95ms/token. But TPS dropped on mixed short requests.

Mateo Alvarez: Quick micro-batching tweak that might help (from bench):
```json
{"batching": {"microbatch_size": 4, "max_latency_ms": 12, "kv_merge_threshold": 256}}
```
Apply kv_merge_threshold to delay dequant until batch assembly — reduces redundant dequant calls.

Priya Rao: Nice. Any numbers after that change?

Alex Chen: After kv_merge_threshold tweak: prefill 2.3ms/token, decode 1.9ms/token, TTFT improvement ~36% vs worst-case. TPS down ~8% for small 1-2 token requests.

Jun Park: Tradeoff makes sense — TTFT vs TPS. For long-context generation, prefer lower TTFT; for high-throughput short completions prefer lower per-request overhead. We should expose policy flag to route by route.

Grace Liu: Agree. Also add an experiment: lazy_dequant_on_prefill=true (dequant only at decode) vs eager_dequant=true. Could be route-configurable.

Mateo Alvarez: I ran lazy_dequant_on_prefill=true experiment overnight on 10 nodes:
```
- observed: prefill_total_ms: 2100 (2.1ms/token), decode_total_ms: 2200 (2.2ms/token)
- notes: decode spikes for first token due to on-demand dequant, but amortized decode ok for long outputs
- regression windows: small requests (<8 tokens) saw 20% worse p99 latency
```

Alex Chen: TL;DR: two knobs: kernel-selection scoring (favor stable kernels for long layers) + batching/kv_merge heuristics. Combined gives ~30% TTFT improvement for long-prefill quantized runs but costs 5-10% TPS on short requests.

Priya Rao: Can we have the console show estimated TTFT vs TPS tradeoff per route so SRE/product can pick defaults?

Grace Liu: I'll add a small dashboard card and a rollout flag. Would like to avoid global default change until we validate on customer traces.

perf-bot: benchmark job 1967890123-mixcalib-sampling completed — full report uploaded to /s3/perf/reports/1967890123-mixcalib.json :thumbsup:

Alex Chen: Thanks — next steps: 1) PR to kernel score tweak (Jun + Mateo), 2) feature flag for kv_merge_threshold (Grace), 3) perf run on customer-like trace (Priya). I'll open tasks and link here.

Priya Rao: I'll queue the trace run and validate p99 impact across routes.

Jun Park: I'll draft the kernel-score change and add unit coverage for selection heuristics.

Grace Liu: I'll wire the flag + dashboard card. We'll gate rollout behind perf acceptance. :white_check_mark:

title: #eng-runtime: 1952346789
preview:
Alice Nguyen: Heads-up — after yesterday's continuous-batcher tweak (mini-window=16 -> 8) we're seeing a 3x p95 spike on short requests (~<=64 tokens). Noticed around 10:45 UTC.
Ben Carter: Confirming, saw it in perf dashboards. Throughput also dipped for small-seq traffic. p50 is fine. p95 and p99 are terrible.
perf-bot: Alert: continuous-batcher-regression detected in prod cluster eu-west-1. Metric: latency_p95_up 320% over baseline. Triggered at 2026-03-18T10:46:12Z.
Carla Mendez: Pushed a targeted profiler run on a replica. Attaching trimmed traces — most samples in "prepare_batch" waiting on queue policy, then kernel selection falls back to slow path.
Dylan Ho: Any clue why kernel selection flips? mini-window smaller -> more batches -> maybe different batch shapes? Could be kernel affinity thresholds.
Alice Nguyen: Here's a minimal repro I ran locally against the canary: ```bash
abatch-client --target=canary:8000 --seq-len=32 --qps=120 --duration=5m
```Results: p95 jump after ~20s.
Frank Li: I pulled a flamegraph snapshot. Key hot path: continuous_batch::form_batches -> check_kernel_compat -> fallback_to_generic_kernel. Lots of contention on kernel_locks.
Eva Singh: Also seeing KV cache churn metrics increase. Cache hit-rate down ~12% for those short requests. Could be because smaller windows break prefix coalescing.
Ben Carter: That matches: smaller mini-window reduces prefix sharing opportunities -> more unique KV keys -> eviction thrash on hosts with limited KV slots.
Carla Mendez: Dump of the profiler snippet: ```
# top functions
30% prepare_batch
22% check_kernel_compat
18% generic_kernel_exec
10% kv_cache_lookup
auth_stats: kernel_lock_waits=0.9ms/op
```We should fix kernel selection early to avoid generic path.
Dylan Ho: Quick fix options: 1) revert mini-window to 16 (rollout revert), 2) tweak kernel affinity to prefer small-seq optimized kernels even if batch shapes vary, 3) raise KV cache capacity on canary.
Alice Nguyen: Can we A/B 2) first? Lower blast radius. If affinity change is safe, we can roll to canary and watch perf-bot.
Frank Li: Affinity tweak: reduce compatibility threshold from 0.85 -> 0.7 so specialized kernels accept slightly more shapes. I'll prepare a small patch.
deploy-bot: Deploy job queued: ref=affinity-tweak-0.7 -> canary (3 nodes). Started by Frank Li at 2026-03-18T11:05:02Z.
perf-bot: Canary metrics: initial 5m look — p95 improved by 45% vs canary before patch. KV hit-rate unchanged immediately.
Eva Singh: After 10m, KV hit-rate creeping back up ~6%. Suggest monitoring eviction rate; if it steadies maybe batching coalescing recovers.
Ben Carter: If affinity tweak holds, we should follow with a slow increase of mini-window shrink in 2 steps instead of 16->8 jump.
Carla Mendez: Agreed. I'll open a short RFC for rollout plan and add a regression test that simulates high small-seq QPS to catch this in CI.
Alice Nguyen: Great. For now let's keep the canary running and hold full rollout. Thanks everyone :thumbsup:

title: #eng-runtime: 1876543210
preview:
Noah Reed: Quick post-RFC sync: proposing two coordinated changes to reduce host fragmentation and cold-page cost: 1) dynamic slab partitioning that adjusts per-tenant slab budgets based on hotness and recent residency, 2) a compressed cold-tier on local NVMe for evicted pages (async compress/decompress). Wants to capture tradeoffs before a PR.

Sana Kaur: High-level +1. Questions: how do we avoid CPU compression starving inference? How do we handle rewarm latency when a compressed page is needed synchronously?

Mateo Alvarez: From the prototype: we used zstd-level 3 for compress on-evict (fast) and a small async compressor pool. Benchmarks (canary host A100 80GB + NVMe):
```
compress level=3: ratio ~3.6x, compress cpu ~2.5ms/page, decompress cpu ~0.9ms/page
median rewarm (with async prefetch): +3% p50, p95: +11% when decompression happens on-path
with prefetch-on-evict-hint (keep last 30s touched pages cached): p95 impact ~+2%
NVMe read latency under load: baseline 1.8ms -> 7-9ms at high concurrency
```
So NVMe and decompression both add tail; prefetch + rate-limits are key.

Noah Reed: Exactly the concerns. I'm thinking: make compression async on eviction; store metadata in ephemeral in-memory index. When an access misses resident memory but finds compressed cold copy, we issue an async fetch+decompress and return a short 202-like signal to client via eviction-notify / rewarm-suggestion so SDKs can retry (or the runtime can block-wait bounded by SLO). UX options: transparent sync decompress vs. cooperative retry.

Sana Kaur: Cooperative retry is nicer for tail control, but harder on SDKs. Could we provide both: runtime-block-policy: {block_sync_ms=50|never}, and SDKs can opt-in to non-blocking. Also need admission control so compression tasks don't saturate CPU.

Mateo Alvarez: Implementation notes: compressor pool sized = min(cores/4, 8) by default; NVMe IO window limited by aio_tokens. Decompression is cheap but still non-zero; if we block on-path we need to cap parallel decompressions to protect GPU threads. We also saw pathological case where many cold pages were requested at once causing NVMe queue storm and p99 blowup. Solution: per-host NVMe queue limiter + per-tenant cold_fetch_budget.

Noah Reed: For fragmentation: dynamic slab partitioning should shrink slab footprints for tenants that demote frequently and inflate for tenants with sustained hotness. Evictor assigns slab slices atomically using a moving-average hotness score. When a slab slice is reclaimed we attempt to coalesce neighbor free buckets. We avoid full compaction except during long idle windows.

Sana Kaur: Important to surface metrics: slab_slice_utilization, per-tenant cold_bytes, compress_ratio, cold_fetch_latency, compressor_pool_util. Add alert: compressor_pool_util>0.8 && cold_fetch_latency_p95>50ms -> throttle compress or reduce canary.

Mateo Alvarez: Also need a correctness story for pinned pages (e.g., models marking pages non-evictable). Pinned pages bypass slab resizing and compression; but we must tie pinned counts to tenant quotas and disallow overcommit. Pinning will be limited behind a tenant quota and FF.

Noah Reed: API sketch for ops/SDKs:
```
// server-side config
set_cold_tier_policy(tenant, compress=true, compression_level=3, compressible=true)
// runtime hint from SDK
eviction_hint(key, prefer_cold=true)
// per-tenant knobs via console
tenant_config.tenant_x.cold_fetch_budget = 10/sec
```
We should avoid surface for basic users; defaults should auto-enable compression for large pages > 64KB.

Sana Kaur: Rollout plan: land dynamic-slab behind FF memory.dynamic_slab=false and cold-tier behind memory.cold_tier=false. Initial canary: 1% hosts with compressed cold-tier read-only (no on-path decompression; runtime returns rewarm suggestion). Then canary 2: allow bounded on-path decompression with block_sync_ms=30 and strict compressor pool limits. Gate on p95, p99, NVMe queue depth, and compressor CPU util.

Mateo Alvarez: I'll add simulations with Q4_replay + spike tests (simultaneous rewarm storm) and tune per-tenant cold_fetch_budget. Also add a backpressure mode where cold fetches are dropped and SDKs receive preemptive eviction-notify with suggested_jittered_backoff.

infra-bot: feature-flag memory.dynamic_slab_compression created. Canary scheduled: group=cold-tier-canary start=Sun 04:00 UTC. Dashboards: /d/memory/cold-tier-compression (owner: @Mateo).

Noah Reed: Action items: Mateo run sims + add NVMe limiter; Sana write RFC appendix on operator metrics and FF gates; I'll draft SDK guidance for cooperative retry patterns and sample code. Keep pinned/sticky disabled until we have quota enforcement. Ping thread when sims land. :+1:

Mateo Alvarez: Will post sim links and perf graphs here by EOD tomorrow.

Sana Kaur: +1

infra-bot: canary created (id: canary-2026-11-18-07).

title: #eng-runtime: 1870001111
preview:
Sam Nguyen: heads up — after yesterday's kernel-selector tweak we're seeing a P50 token latency regression on /chat/large. Not huge but reproducible in canary and in a subset of prod requests. dashboard: https://graf.redwood.internal/d/uxr3k/multi-route-overview

Lee Ortega: saw that too. SM occupancy dropped on slice-2 and slice-4 for the affected hosts, VRAM usage nudged up ~200MB, and power draw pulses when the batcher kicks. linking a quick screenshot: https://img.redwood.internal/s/shot123.png :thinking_face:

Priya Rao: metric snippets I pulled from prometheus this morning:```
# avg token latency by route
sum(rate(token_latency_seconds_sum{route="/chat/large"}[5m])) / sum(rate(token_latency_seconds_count{route="/chat/large"}[5m]))
# sm occupancy by MIG slice
avg(nvidia_smi_sm_occupancy{mig_slice=~"slice-[2|4]"})
```

Mateo Alvarez: possible root cause: kernel-selector started preferring single-stream kernels for certain sequence-length distributions, which increases VRAM working set and prevents effective KV reuse for batched prefixes. Essentially more eviction churn in the KV cache.

Sam Nguyen: can we correlate KV cache evictions with request traces? I saw increased eviction_rate on the same hosts. If eviction spikes right before token latency spikes, that's our smoking gun.

Lee Ortega: I dumped a small tail sample from canary host-17:```
EVICT key=ctx-482 len=2048 age=32ms
KVCACHE_LOOKUP hit=false route=/chat/large req_id=abc123
```
That aligns with the latency waterfall — lots of KV misses causing full re-encode.

Priya Rao: added a temporary panel to the perf dashboard: "kv_evictions / token latency (per-host)". dashboard-bot posted the link and updated the dashboard version. dashboard-bot: Updated dashboard ux/perf_kv_evictions (v12) - panels added: kv_evictions_heatmap, evict_vs_token_latency.

dashboard-bot: New panel URLs: https://graf.redwood.internal/d/uxr3k/perf_kv_evictions?panelId=7 and https://graf.redwood.internal/d/uxr3k/perf_kv_evictions?panelId=8 :white_check_mark:

Mateo Alvarez: quick repro script I ran in canary:```bash
# simulate chat/large with varied prefix lengths
for L in 16 64 128 256; do
  seq 1 50 | xargs -P4 -I{} curl -s -X POST https://canary-api.internal/generate -d '{"route":"/chat/large","prefix_len":'$L'}'
done
```

Sam Nguyen: thanks. plan: roll kernel-selector rollback on canary (20% traffic) and run the repro for 30m, watch kv_evictions & sm_occupancy. If stable, stage rollout to the rest. Priya — can you keep the kv_evictions panel on 30s refresh for the next 6h?

Priya Rao: will do. set to 30s and pinned to the ENG_RUNTIME dashboard group. also added an alert rule: kv_evictions_rate > 50/min AND token_p50 > 0.12s for 5m -> page oncall.

oncall-bot: Alert rule created: EVCT-LAT-REGRESSION. Oncall: @Lee Ortega will be paged for priority-2 alerts.

Lee Ortega: i'm on it. starting the canary rollback now and will post logs. Expect a 3–5 minute window for kube rolling.

Sam Nguyen: appreciate it. If the rollback fixes it, we'll keep the kernel-selector change off until we can add a KV-aware cost term to the selector. That selector needs to consider predicted working set size and kv-hit probability, not just raw kernel throughput.

Mateo Alvarez: Agreed. I can prototype a small estimator: use recent prefix length distribution + route model to estimate KV hit ratio and penalize kernels that increase working set. Will spike a PR for an experimental flag.

Sam Nguyen: perfect. keep messages short in this thread so we have a clean audit trail.

```

Mateo Alvarez: possible root cause: kernel-selector started preferring single-stream kernels for certain sequence-length distributions, which increases VRAM working set and prevents effective KV reuse for batched prefixes. Essentially more eviction churn in the KV cache.

Sam Nguyen: can we correlate KV cache evictions with request traces? I saw increased eviction_rate on the same hosts. If eviction spikes right before token latency spikes, that's our smoking gun.

Lee Ortega: I dumped a small tail sample from canary host-17:```
EVICT key=ctx-482 len=2048 age=32ms
KVCACHE_LOOKUP hit=false route=/chat/large req_id=abc123
```
That aligns with the latency waterfall — lots of KV misses causing full re-encode.

Priya Rao: added a temporary panel to the perf dashboard: "kv_evictions / token latency (per-host)". dashboard-bot posted the link and updated the dashboard version. dashboard-bot: Updated dashboard ux/perf_kv_evictions (v12) - panels added: kv_evictions_heatmap, evict_vs_token_latency.

dashboard-bot: New panel URLs: https://graf.redwood.internal/d/uxr3k/perf_kv_evictions?panelId=7 and https://graf.redwood.internal/d/uxr3k/perf_kv_evictions?panelId=8 :white_check_mark:

Mateo Alvarez: quick repro script I ran in canary:```bash
# simulate chat/large with varied prefix lengths
for L in 16 64 128 256; do
  seq 1 50 | xargs -P4 -I{} curl -s -X POST https://canary-api.internal/generate -d '{"route":"/chat/large","prefix_len":'$L'}'
done
```

Sam Nguyen: thanks. plan: roll kernel-selector rollback on canary (20% traffic) and run the repro for 30m, watch kv_evictions & sm_occupancy. If stable, stage rollout to the rest. Priya — can you keep the kv_evictions panel on 30s refresh for the next 6h?

Priya Rao: will do. set to 30s and pinned to the ENG_RUNTIME dashboard group. also added an alert rule: kv_evictions_rate > 50/min AND token_p50 > 0.12s for 5m -> page oncall.

oncall-bot: Alert rule created: EVCT-LAT-REGRESSION. Oncall: @Lee Ortega will be paged for priority-2 alerts.

Lee Ortega: i'm on it. starting the canary rollback now and will post logs. Expect a 3–5 minute window for kube rolling.

Sam Nguyen: appreciate it. If the rollback fixes it, we'll keep the kernel-selector change off until we can add a KV-aware cost term to the selector. That selector needs to consider predicted working set size and kv-hit probability, not just raw kernel throughput.

Mateo Alvarez: Agreed. I can prototype a small estimator: use recent prefix length distribution + route model to estimate KV hit ratio and penalize kernels that increase working set. Will spike a PR for an experimental flag.

Sam Nguyen: perfect. keep messages short in this thread so we have a clean audit trail.

title: #eng-runtime: 1799954321
preview:
Ava Chen: Ran a focused probe for streaming vs non-streaming using the new kernel-probe flags. Goal: understand kernel selection points, batching heuristics, and KV cache churn under chunked responses. Short summary & raw numbers below, please re-run or comment if you can.

Miguel Torres: Which model/infra/resolution? gpus?

Ava Chen: 2.7B open model on A10G pool (single zone), runtime=release-2026-11. Probe toggled prefer-fused, fallback-matmul, and kernel-switch threshold at token=128. Harness: replay v2 with synthetic client that supports chunking and pacing.

Riley Park: Cool. Did you capture p50/p95/p99 + qps + host cpu?

Miguel Torres: From the primary run (chunk_size=64, no client pacing, warmup 5m): streaming => p50=48ms p95=210ms p99=380ms, qps=1180, host CPU=62%. Non-streaming => p50=65ms p95=320ms p99=560ms, qps=1350, host CPU=44%.

Ava Chen: TL;DR: streaming wins tails but costs CPU and reduces batching efficiency due to client-side backpressure and more frequent kernel switches mid-request.

perf-bot: Perf job 1799954321-rt uploaded: https://ci.internal/runs/1799954321 - includes flamegraph (kernel/cpu), kernel-switch timelines, and KV-cache heatmap. Summary: KV hit-rate dropped ~9% on streaming runs because partial-chunk writes cause earlier segment evictions.

Riley Park: That KV observation matches what I expected. Streaming breaks contiguous prefix access so KV segments get less reuse. Did you try pinning prefixes in the cache?

Miguel Torres: Quick experiment: pin TTL=30s for prefix segments -> streaming p95 improved from 210ms -> 200ms and qps 1180 -> 1220, but memory usage +12%. Pin TTL=120s further improves qps but memory spike unacceptable on small nodes.

Ava Chen: Noted. Batching heuristics: continuous batching (streaming) had avg batch size ~12 requests, non-streaming ~24. So non-streaming packs better which explains the qps advantage.

Riley Park: Client readloop behavior matters too. With chunked responses we saw tight readloop syscall wakeups; that causes CPU sys% to spike and increases context switches. Did you try adding pacing in client loop?

Miguel Torres: Yes. Re-run with chunk_size=128 and client pacing=0.5ms: streaming p95=190ms, qps=1250, host CPU=55% (much better). So pacing reduces syscall storms and improves batching backpressure.

Ava Chen: Great. Also watched kernel selection logs: a bunch of mid-request switches when seq_len crosses 128 boundary due to dynamic selection logic. That increases overhead because we rebind kernels and sometimes fall back to matmul path temporarily.

Riley Park: We should try prefer-fused=true so we bias toward fused-attn earlier. Might reduce mid-request oscillation.

Miguel Torres: Tried prefer-fused=true on a stab: p95 improved ~8-12ms on streaming, kernel-switch events reduced by ~30%. Tradeoff: fused path uses slightly more vram.

Ava Chen: Action items from this thread:
- expose small TTL knob for prefix pinning and test 30/60/120s in matrix
- add client pacing option to replay harness and test 0.25/0.5/1.0ms
- run kernel-probe with prefer-fused toggled and record switch counts
- ensure warmup step (10m) before measuring to get stable KV warm cache

infra-bot: queued replay run: replay-job-1799954321-2 (cmd: replay --id=1799954321 --chunk=128 --pacing=0.5 --pin-ttl=30 --warmup=600). ETA ~45m.

Riley Park: For apples-to-apples we also need same batch targets and same concurrency envelope. Warm cache is key — add explicit warm trace run with identical trace and clients.

Ava Chen: Agreed. I'll update the harness and schedule a nightly matrix: chunk_size=[64,128,256], pacing=[0,0.5], pin_ttl=[0,30]. Also add A100 vs A10G comparison per Miguel's ask.

Miguel Torres: If anyone has spare A100 time, please flag. Curious if kernel selection differs enough to change the prefer-fused recommendation.

Ava Chen: Adding A100 to the run matrix. Thanks all :rocket:

Riley Park: FYI leaving a tiny note here: consider adding a kernel-probe flag to log per-request kernel decision points (lightweight) — saved us debugging time.

Ava Chen: Will add probe-logging opt. Closing after runs complete and I'll post consolidated numbers + graphs in #eng-runtime.

title: #eng-runtime: 1776460021
preview:
Connor O'Brien: For runtime 1.22.0 we need to lock down *default flag states* (what ships in the release image) + how we document internal overrides (canary/region/model). Can folks weigh in by EOD so release notes + config docs match reality?

Connor O'Brien: Flags in scope (from the sprint):
- `runtime.kernel.fp8_attention.enabled`
- `runtime.kernel.int8_matmul.enabled`
- `runtime.kernel.autoselect_fp8_int8.enabled`
- `runtime.scheduler.mixed_prefill_decode_v2.enabled`
- `runtime.perf_canary.gate_quant_kernels.enabled`
- `runtime.metrics.tagging.model_gpu_kernel_mode.enabled`

Hiro Tanaka: For kernel side, my strong preference is:
- ship binaries with the code paths present
- keep `fp8_attention` + `int8_matmul` default OFF
- `autoselect_fp8_int8` default OFF as well, otherwise we’re effectively turning on kernels via heuristic.

Hiro Tanaka: We can still enable per model/GPU/region via server-side flags + perf canary gate. We just don’t want “silent global enable” the minute the image rolls.

Sofia Ivanova: +1 on cautious defaults. For scheduler, `mixed_prefill_decode_v2` default OFF in 1.22.0.

Sofia Ivanova: We saw good end-to-end wins with it ON, but it changes queueing behavior. I’d rather run canary cohort first so SRE has clean rollback lever.

Noah Patel: Agree. Default OFF is safest for all “behavioral” changes.

Noah Patel: I do think `metrics.tagging.model_gpu_kernel_mode` should be default ON though. If we don’t ship that on by default, we’ll lose the ability to attribute regressions once people flip kernels.

Allison Grant: +1 metrics tagging ON. Dashboards are wired assuming those labels exist (`model`, `gpu_sku`, `kernel_mode`, `quant_mode`). If it’s OFF, canary diffs get messy.

Rafael Mendes: From SRE side:
- `perf_canary.gate_quant_kernels` should be ON by default *in canary envs*, but I’m ok with default OFF in “prod stable” if we’re explicit in the runbook.
- We need a single source of truth for “what’s the default in the image” vs “what we override in the control plane.” This bit keeps confusing people during incidents.

Connor O'Brien: Got it. We can separate:
(A) image defaults (what ships)
(B) control plane overrides (what we actually enable per cohort)

Rina Kobayashi: For harness runs, we’ve been using:
- baseline: all quant flags OFF
- variant: `autoselect_fp8_int8` ON + allowlist of shapes
So shipping defaults OFF matches the benchmark framing (makes claims easier).

Grace Kim: One nuance: INT8 matmul path is “safe” for a lot of shapes but still has shape guards + fallback. Still, I’m fine default OFF. The moment it’s ON globally, we’ll get weird edge cases from long-tail models.

Julian Moore: Scheduler instrumentation flags aren’t listed here but FYI they’re already default ON in 1.21.x (`prefill_decode_split_metrics`). No changes needed.

Connor O'Brien: Re: perf canary gate default, can we do: image default ON, but only active when canary controller is present? Or is that too magic.

Rafael Mendes: Please no magic.

Rafael Mendes: Keep `perf_canary.gate_quant_kernels` default OFF in image, and we set it ON explicitly in the canary deployment config (Helm values / control plane flag). During an incident, we want to be able to say “this service has gate on/off” without reading code.

Allison Grant: +1. Also makes staging vs prod comparisons clean.

Noah Patel: For documentation: can we put a short table in the internal runtime flags doc + link the rollout runbook? Something like “Default (image)” + “Recommended enablement path.”

Yuki Sato: We already have a doc PR in flight (PR-19903) adding flag names + safe rollout. I can add the default-state table there.

Connor O'Brien: Perfect. Can you also call out the override mechanism? (control plane key + per-model allowlist). The question I keep getting is “I set flag X, why didn’t it flip?” because allowlists block it.

Yuki Sato: Yep. I’ll add:
- flag name
- default
- what else must be true (GPU support, shape guard, allowlist)
- where override is set (control plane)

Hiro Tanaka: Please include a warning that `autoselect_fp8_int8` doesn’t mean “always FP8/INT8” — it means “eligible shapes pick quant kernel else fallback.” People interpret it wrong.

Sofia Ivanova: And similarly for scheduler: `mixed_prefill_decode_v2` changes batching window/backpressure; should only be enabled w/ the tuned params from PR-19911, otherwise it’s not representative.

Connor O'Brien: OK, proposing final defaults for 1.22.0 image:
- `runtime.kernel.fp8_attention.enabled` = OFF
- `runtime.kernel.int8_matmul.enabled` = OFF
- `runtime.kernel.autoselect_fp8_int8.enabled` = OFF
- `runtime.scheduler.mixed_prefill_decode_v2.enabled` = OFF
- `runtime.perf_canary.gate_quant_kernels.enabled` = OFF
- `runtime.metrics.tagging.model_gpu_kernel_mode.enabled` = ON

Connor O'Brien: Enablement plan (not image defaults): canary config flips gate + selectively enables kernels per model/GPU/region.

Rafael Mendes: Works for me.

Allison Grant: Works. I’ll double check dashboards don’t require any other default-on flags.

Hiro Tanaka: +1.

Sofia Ivanova: +1.

Rina Kobayashi: +1.

Grace Kim: +1.

Connor O'Brien: Action items:
- @Yuki Sato update PR-19903 with the default-state table + override notes (allowlists/guards). Link runbook.
- @Rafael Mendes confirm canary Helm values set `perf_canary.gate_quant_kernels` ON + document in the runbook.
- I’ll update release notes draft to reflect “shipped behind flags, default OFF; enabled via staged rollout.”

Connor O'Brien: If anyone objects, reply here by 5pm PT. Otherwise I’ll mark this as final for the RC.

ReleaseBot: runtime-1.22.0-rc3 built from `redwood@9f3c2b1`. Artifact: `gs://redwood-releases/runtime/1.22.0-rc3`
