title: #eng-runtime: 1770383607
preview:
Noah Patel: seeing a big gap between staging and prod variance on the canary runs. staging p95 is basically flat, prod p95 is bouncing way more. before we lock thresholds, can we sanity check what “normal” looks like in prod?

Rina Kobayashi: +1. staging harness run from this morning (same seed + same shapes) had p95 within ~2-3% across 6h. prod shadow in us-east was ~10-15% swing depending on hour.

Sofia Ivanova: prod has real queueing and batch dynamics that staging doesn’t. if we set thresholds from staging variance we’re gonna page constantly.

Omar El-Sayed: do we have the breakdown on where variance comes from? e2e vs runtime-only? (i.e., queue + scheduler vs kernel execution)

Nadia Rahman: I pulled a quick compare. Same model + same canary profile.
- staging: runtime_only_p95_ms stddev ~1.8ms on a mean ~62ms
- prod (us-east): runtime_only_p95_ms stddev ~4-5ms on a mean ~65ms
- prod e2e_p95_ms stddev ~11-14ms on a mean ~92ms
Queueing_time is the big delta.

Noah Patel: ok that matches my gut. canary is sensitive to “system” variance in prod (autoscaler, fleet mix, background load). question is: do we want canary alerting to include that? some regressions we care about are exactly queueing/scheduler.

Sofia Ivanova: yes, but we need to separate “scheduler regression” from “fleet got weird for an hour”. otherwise it’s noise + no actionable owner.

Logan Wright: prod variance also correlates with telemetry ingestion delays (we saw 2-5m lag spikes). not causing latency, but it makes the regression evaluator look jumpy if we’re sampling too tight.

Hiro Tanaka: kernel-side regressions shouldn’t show up as big queueing deltas. if runtime_only moves + queueing stable, that’s a better kernel signal. if only e2e moves, likely infra/scheduler.

Omar El-Sayed: can we update the evaluator to gate on both?
- “runtime regression” => runtime_only_p95 delta > X%
- “system regression” => e2e_p95 delta > Y% AND queueing_p95 delta > Z%
Then route differently.

Noah Patel: yeah. but thresholds need to reflect prod variance. right now the draft says warn at +10% p95. staging makes that feel fine, prod makes it too twitchy.

Sean Gallagher: from oncall side: if warn-level is noisy it gets ignored. paging must be super conservative. we can allow warn to be “maybe” but it needs dampening + clear attribution or it’s just spam.

Rina Kobayashi: for prod, the canary is only ~0.2% capacity budget right now. so sample sizes are lower too. p95 on low N is just inherently noisy.

Nadia Rahman: exactly. if we’re using histograms, quantile stability improves once we’re at a few hundred requests in-window. right now some models only hit ~80-120 samples per 10m window.

Noah Patel: proposal:
- evaluator window: 30m for warn, 60m for page
- min_samples: warn >= 300, page >= 800 (per model/region/SKU)
- warn if e2e_p95 +15% OR tps -12% sustained across 2 consecutive windows
- page if (top models only) runtime_only_p95 +20% OR tps -20% sustained across 2 windows

Sofia Ivanova: +1, and add “queueing attribution” in the alert payload:
- if queueing_p95 accounts for >50% of delta, mark as likely scheduler/infra
- else likely runtime/kernel

Hiro Tanaka: can we also include kernel tag in the payload? if runtime_only regresses and kernel_tag changed recently, that’s instant signal.

Omar El-Sayed: kernel_tag is in runtime telemetry now (from PR-19193). canary service needs to forward it on the alert webhook payload. I can add.

Logan Wright: careful on cardinality. don’t put full git sha as a label. but including a single “runtime_build_id” string in the payload is fine (payload != metric labels).

Sean Gallagher: for thresholds, I’d strongly suggest per-region thresholds if we know eu-west is inherently noisier. otherwise we’ll keep arguing about “prod is fine” while paging.

Nadia Rahman: fwiw eu-west variance is ~1.3x us-east on e2e. runtime_only is similar. so it’s mostly queueing/fleet.

Noah Patel: ok so staged plan:
1) keep staging thresholds tight for pre-merge sanity (separate from prod)
2) prod warn uses higher thresholds + min samples
3) prod page only on runtime_only signal (plus top models list)
4) add payload fields: queueing_share_of_delta, runtime_build_id, kernel_tag, fleet_sku

Rina Kobayashi: sounds good. also: staging shouldn’t be compared to prod baselines at all. staging baselines should be computed in staging env (otherwise it’ll “regress” every time).

Sofia Ivanova: +1000. staging is for catching obvious kernel/scheduler bugs. prod is for catching real regressions before customers.

Hiro Tanaka: last note: if we set page on runtime_only +20%, we should verify that real kernel regressions show up that big. some are like +8-12% but still painful.

Noah Patel: fair. maybe page threshold depends on model criticality. top-3 models page at +15%, rest +20%. let’s calibrate with baseline results.

Nadia Rahman: I’ll drop a quick table in the calibration sheet w/ observed prod variance bands so we can pick numbers that are >2 sigma of normal.

Sean Gallagher: please also update runbook text to say “staging variance is not representative; don’t use it to argue prod is broken”. this keeps coming up in incidents.

Noah Patel: ack. I’ll incorporate in the ADR + runbook. thanks all.

- “system regression” => e2e_p95 delta > Y% AND queueing_p95 delta > Z%
Then route differently.

Noah Patel: yeah. but thresholds need to reflect prod variance. right now the draft says warn at +10% p95. staging makes that feel fine, prod makes it too twitchy.

Sean Gallagher: from oncall side: if warn-level is noisy it gets ignored. paging must be super conservative. we can allow warn to be “maybe” but it needs dampening + clear attribution or it’s just spam.

Rina Kobayashi: for prod, the canary is only ~0.2% capacity budget right now. so sample sizes are lower too. p95 on low N is just inherently noisy.

Nadia Rahman: exactly. if we’re using histograms, quantile stability improves once we’re at a few hundred requests in-window. right now some models only hit ~80-120 samples per 10m window.

Noah Patel: proposal:
- evaluator window: 30m for warn, 60m for page
- min_samples: warn >= 300, page >= 800 (per model/region/SKU)
- warn if e2e_p95 +15% OR tps -12% sustained across 2 consecutive windows
- page if (top models only) runtime_only_p95 +20% OR tps -20% sustained across 2 windows

Sofia Ivanova: +1, and add “queueing attribution” in the alert payload:
- if queueing_p95 accounts for >50% of delta, mark as likely scheduler/infra
- else likely runtime/kernel

Hiro Tanaka: can we also include kernel tag in the payload? if runtime_only regresses and kernel_tag changed recently, that’s instant signal.

Omar El-Sayed: kernel_tag is in runtime telemetry now (from PR-19193). canary service needs to forward it on the alert webhook payload. I can add.

Logan Wright: careful on cardinality. don’t put full git sha as a label. but including a single “runtime_build_id” string in the payload is fine (payload != metric labels).

Sean Gallagher: for thresholds, I’d strongly suggest per-region thresholds if we know eu-west is inherently noisier. otherwise we’ll keep arguing about “prod is fine” while paging.

Nadia Rahman: fwiw eu-west variance is ~1.3x us-east on e2e. runtime_only is similar. so it’s mostly queueing/fleet.

Noah Patel: ok so staged plan:
1) keep staging thresholds tight for pre-merge sanity (separate from prod)
2) prod warn uses higher thresholds + min samples
3) prod page only on runtime_only signal (plus top models list)
4) add payload fields: queueing_share_of_delta, runtime_build_id, kernel_tag, fleet_sku

Rina Kobayashi: sounds good. also: staging shouldn’t be compared to prod baselines at all. staging baselines should be computed in staging env (otherwise it’ll “regress” every time).

Sofia Ivanova: +1000. staging is for catching obvious kernel/scheduler bugs. prod is for catching real regressions before customers.

Hiro Tanaka: last note: if we set page on runtime_only +20%, we should verify that real kernel regressions show up that big. some are like +8-12% but still painful.

Noah Patel: fair. maybe page threshold depends on model criticality. top-3 models page at +15%, rest +20%. let’s calibrate with baseline results.

Nadia Rahman: I’ll drop a quick table in the calibration sheet w/ observed prod variance bands so we can pick numbers that are >2 sigma of normal.

Sean Gallagher: please also update runbook text to say “staging variance is not representative; don’t use it to argue prod is broken”. this keeps coming up in incidents.

Noah Patel: ack. I’ll incorporate in the ADR + runbook. thanks all.

title: #eng-runtime: 1770213309
preview:
Talia Benson: GA check for kernel auto-selection v2 (runtime 1.21): can we confirm all GA criteria met + get final approvals? Looking for: perf deltas, error rates, denylist status, SLO gates, and rollout readiness. If you’re a required approver, pls reply with ✅ / 🚫 + any last caveats.

Marcus Reed: Perf summary (Hosted canary + perf-suite):
- p95 latency: -6.8% overall weighted across canary scenarios (biggest win on A100 + short/med decode)
- p99 latency: -0.9% overall; within gate (no sustained tail regressions)
- Throughput: +4.1% tokens/s at same concurrency; variance down (less jitter in mixed workloads)
- Long-context (16k/32k) after ENG fix (PR-28522): no regression vs v1 in latest run; p99 recovered.
Numbers are in the perf results sheet + perf-canary dashboards. ✅ from perf side.

Omar El-Sayed: Tail latency + errors from observability:
- Error rate: flat. No increase in timeouts/ooms attributable to selector. The only bump we saw during 25% rollout was the L40S long-context misselect, resolved w/ threshold tweak + denylist entry.
- p99 guardrail: green in the last 48h across us-east + eu-west. No new spikes in the “selection_delta_p99” panel.
- Telemetry overhead: sampling at 0.5% in prod; selector eval cost within microbench bound.
✅ from me.

Allison Grant: SRE/SLO gates check:
- slo-toolkit gates passed for Hosted regions (p95/p99 budgets, error rate thresholds).
- Alerts: updated to account for new selector tags; we’re not seeing the cardinality blow-up from the earlier false positive (INT-10411 is effectively resolved once the query change landed).
- Rollback triggers + playbook verified.
✅

Aisha Bello: +1 on monitoring. Dashboards look good:
- Selection distribution stable (no weird drift by model family/seq bucket)
- “Unknown/other” bucket < 0.2% after SKU normalization fix
- Canary criteria met per doc
✅

Yuki Sato: Selector correctness/determinism:
- Deterministic replay tests in CI green (PR-28473). Same inputs -> same selected kernel.
- Feature extraction + seq regime bucketing stable; scheduler hint integration (PR-28502) is enabled but only influences tie-breaks.
- Fallback hierarchy behaving as designed (no missing-kernel crashes; clean fallback to baseline kernel).
✅

Hiro Tanaka: Kernels/denylist status:
- Denylist reviewed + up to date for known-bad combos (L40S + driver 550.54 for one fused prefill path; plus a couple A100 MIG edge cases). Those are blocked in registry.
- Guardrail disable v2 on unstable driver/CUDA combos (PR-28495) is in.
- Kernel registry coverage: all GA model families covered w/ at least one fast path + baseline fallback.
✅ from kernels.

Benji Okafor: Control plane gating:
- Allowlist/denylist rules live + hot reload tested.
- Default: v2 on for Hosted for approved models; Dedicated remains opt-in until Dedicated signoff window.
- We can disable by model/GPU/region in <5 min if needed.
✅

Ethan Park: +1. Gate config + persistence/migrations look good. We validated config propagation in staging + 2 prod regions.
✅

Jordan Lee: Policy engine integration is stable; no nondeterministic rule ordering issues observed after last patch. ✅

Sahana Iyer: CI + release health:
- GPU perf/regression jobs green for 3 consecutive runs.
- No new flakes from determinism tests.
- Runtime 1.21 candidate is tagged, artifacts ready.
✅

ReleaseBot: Release status update: runtime-1.21.0-rc3 has passed required checks. Pending: final GA approval + deploy window confirmation.

Connor O'Brien: Thanks all. From Release Eng: ✅ to GA for Hosted.
Plan:
- Flip Hosted to 100% for allowlisted models after the deploy window (today 18:00–19:00 PT)
- Keep denylist enforced + telemetry sampling unchanged
- Dedicated: start 10% tomorrow AM in dedicated-a100 pool, then expand if green (Lauren/Markus aligned offline)
If any objections, speak now; otherwise I’ll mark ENG-4821 phase as GA-ready.

Talia Benson: Amazing. Marking GA criteria met + logging approvals in the epic + checklist. Connor, please proceed w/ Hosted GA per plan. Oncall note: rollback authority is Release Eng + SRE (per Mateo thread). :thumbsup:

Omar El-Sayed: (reaction :thumbsup:)

Allison Grant: (reaction :white_check_mark:)

Hiro Tanaka: (reaction :thumbsup:)

Yuki Sato: I’ll stay on for the window in case anything weird shows up in selection distribution.

Connor O'Brien: 👍 will post in #eng-releases when rollout starts + when we hit 100%.

title: #eng-runtime: 1769968421
preview:
Sofia Ivanova: Kickoff thread for mixed-workload scheduler prototype. Goal is to get initial short/long thresholds + owners so Lena can start the design doc. If you’ve looked at traces already, drop your numbers/links here.

Lena Fischer: :wave: I’ll drive the design doc + proposed policy (two-queue + aging). Need input on (a) classification threshold(s) and (b) what we can reliably compute at enqueue time (prompt tokens vs predicted prefill cost). Also who’s owning benchmarks/trace replay?

Claire Dubois: I pulled a quick distro from last week’s anonymized Hosted chat traces (sample ~2M req). Roughly: median prompt ~120 tok, p90 ~700, p95 ~1.6k, p99 ~6.2k. Long tail is heavy. My take: “short” cutoff somewhere 256–384 toks, and maybe treat >2048 as definitely long.

Claire Dubois: Link to working sheet (WIP): https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1rwi-mixed-scheduler (see “mixed-workload-trace-analysis-sheet”). I’ll tighten numbers + add Dedicated traces by EOD tomorrow.

Marcus Reed: Thanks. Q: are those prompt tokens only, or prompt+context? We should be careful because long-context chats have small new prompt but huge kv.

Claire Dubois: Good call. Those are input tokens for request payload (prompt). I can add “effective context” proxy using request’s reported `prefill_tokens` from runtime logs where available.

Omar El-Sayed: On the runtime side we *do* have `kv_cache_tokens_in_use` per request once it’s in the scheduler, but at enqueue we usually only know prompt length + max_new_tokens. For long-context, prompt length is already large though.

Lena Fischer: For v0, I’m leaning on prompt_tokens as primary (stable + already plumbed), and optionally a predicted prefill cost if we can cheaply estimate: `prefill_tokens = prompt_tokens` (or prompt+prefix cache miss). Keep it simple.

Julian Moore: For instrumentation: if we do 2 classes, please make sure we emit per-class queue depth + time-in-queue + dispatch share. I can take that as my workstream, but I’ll need final class labels + any sub-reason codes (e.g., “long_by_prompt”, “long_by_predicted_prefill”).

Sofia Ivanova: +1. Also we need starvation counters / max wait guardrails for SRE signoff.

Sean Gallagher: From SRE angle: I don’t care which cutoff you pick as long as (a) it’s configurable per pool, (b) we can see per-class p95/TTFT, and (c) long requests aren’t unbounded. Prefer explicit “aging” parameter + metric.

Rina Kobayashi: I can own benchmark suite changes. We already have a harness for 80/20 mixes; I’ll add standardized scenarios (80/20, 50/50, long-spike). Need your first-pass cutoff so I can generate workloads around it.

Lena Fischer: Proposed initial cutoffs to try in benchmarks:
- short if `prompt_tokens <= 256`
- long if `prompt_tokens >= 1024`
- middle bucket goes to “short” by default but with token budget caps (so it can’t starve long)
Thoughts?

Marcus Reed: 256 seems reasonable for chat. 1024 long threshold maybe too low? But fine if it’s just for “always long”. Middle bucket defaulting to short might be risky (a lot of 700–900 tok prompts) -> could starve true long.

Claire Dubois: In the sheet, p90 is ~700, so if we treat <=256 as “short”, that’s maybe ~60–65% of traffic. If you treat <=512 as short, you capture closer to ~75–80%. So I’d add 512 as another candidate. For “always long”, maybe 2048 aligns with p95-ish.

Omar El-Sayed: +1 to testing 512. Also decode cost dominates for some requests even if prompt is short but max_new_tokens is huge. Are we considering output length?

Lena Fischer: For v0 classification, I’d avoid max_new_tokens (clients set it high even if they stop early). But we can add a safeguard: if `max_new_tokens > X` then treat as long class or apply stricter token budget.

Benji Okafor: Config plumbing is straightforward if we keep it to a few knobs. Suggest:
- `scheduler.classification.short_prompt_tokens_max`
- `scheduler.classification.long_prompt_tokens_min`
- `scheduler.aging.max_wait_ms`
- `scheduler.budget.short_tokens_per_iter` / `long_tokens_per_iter`
All per pool + defaults per model.

Julian Moore: For metrics naming, I’ll align with existing runtime metrics:
- `scheduler_queue_depth{class=short|long}`
- `scheduler_time_in_queue_ms_bucket{class=...}`
- `scheduler_dispatch_tokens_total{class=...}`
- `scheduler_starvation_events_total{class=...}`
If we later add “medium”, it’s fine.

Sofia Ivanova: OK on owners:
- Design doc + ADR: @Lena Fischer (reviewers: Noah Patel + Sean)
- Trace analysis + threshold recs: @Claire Dubois (loop in Omar for context proxy)
- Benchmarks: @Rina Kobayashi (support: Claire)
- Implementation (classification + two-queue): @Marcus Reed (support: Benji on config)
- Instrumentation: @Julian Moore

Sofia Ivanova: Action items for this week:
1) Claire: post updated sheet w/ Dedicated + context proxy
2) Lena: draft design sections: classification + two-queue + aging + token budgets
3) Rina: draft benchmark plan + initial mixes (80/20, 50/50)
4) Marcus: spike branch for classification hooks

Lena Fischer: Works. I’ll start the Confluence page today and drop link here once created.

Claire Dubois: I’ll update with 3 candidate cutoff sets for benchmarking:
A) short<=256 / long>=2048
B) short<=384 / long>=2048
C) short<=512 / long>=2048
…and include expected traffic split.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: please include rollback criteria placeholders in the design doc (p95 drift limits, long queue max wait). We’ll formalize later.

Marcus Reed: Cool. I’ll also add a quick sim with A/B/C cutoffs if I can. If anyone has an existing queueing sim notebook, send.

Rina Kobayashi: I have an old SRPT vs FIFO sim notebook from last quarter. I’ll DM you.

Sofia Ivanova: Great. Keep all updates in this thread for now so we can link it in the Linear initiative.

title: #eng-runtime: 1769877202
preview:
Noah Patel: quick q for perf-canary attribution: should we add a KV cache hit-rate signal to the canary metrics? thinking it might help explain p95 shifts where runtime-only looks flat but e2e latency moves (or vice versa).

Sofia Ivanova: do you mean KV cache like prefix/KV reuse across requests? (not the per-request kv cache that’s always there)

Noah Patel: yeah, the cross-request prefix cache / reuse. the one that gets better/worse depending on cache policy + eviction behavior.

Omar El-Sayed: technically we can expose it. runtime already knows when a request had a cache hit vs miss, plus how many tokens reused.

Hiro Tanaka: what’s the hypothesis? regressions we’ve seen from cache changes have been mostly memory pressure -> more evictions -> queueing/batching changes, not direct kernel perf.

Noah Patel: exactly. today canary screams “p95 up” and we’re guessing: scheduler? infra? cache? if we have a stable cache hit ratio + reused_tokens, we can say “hit-rate cratered” and stop blaming kernels.

Logan Wright: adding a metric is fine, but careful on labels/cardinality. do NOT do per-prefix or anything derived from prompt. should be aggregate counters/gauges.

Nadia Rahman: +1. also: what would “hit-rate” mean in canary? our current synthetic prompts are mostly random-ish shapes. we’ll get ~0% hits unless we intentionally repeat prefixes.

Rina Kobayashi: canary prompt generator right now does vary by bucket but we don’t repeat the same exact prefix often. we could add a “fixed system prefix” mode so we exercise the prefix cache similarly to prod (lots of apps have stable system prompt).

Sofia Ivanova: but do we *want* to exercise prefix cache? there’s a school of thought that canary should measure raw runtime throughput independent of caching. otherwise if cache hit rate goes up we get a “performance improvement” that isn’t from runtime.

Noah Patel: fair. i’m not proposing we gate on it (at least not v1). more like attribution-only panel + included in alert payload if it changed a lot.

Omar El-Sayed: implementation options:
- counter: `kv_prefix_cache_requests_total{hit="true|false", model=...}`
- counter: `kv_prefix_cache_reused_tokens_total{model=...}`
- gauge: `kv_prefix_cache_hit_ratio` computed in canary aggregator (avoid a new timeseries per label?)

Logan Wright: prefer counters + compute ratio in queries. gauges tend to flap if scrape aligns weird. counters w/ rate() are stable.

Nadia Rahman: also we should split “cache enabled” vs “cache disabled” explicitly. in some tiers it’s off.

Sofia Ivanova: +1. if canary runs against both hosted + dedicated w different cache policies, the hit-rate will be apples/oranges.

Noah Patel: can label `cache_policy` with low-card values? like `off|on_default|on_aggressive`.

Logan Wright: label cardinality ok if it’s a tiny enum controlled by config. make sure it’s validated (control plane) so someone can’t put arbitrary strings.

Hiro Tanaka: what’s the action item? i don’t want this to delay the current alert tuning.

Noah Patel: proposing:
1) add counters in runtime telemetry (or from canary side if we already get it in response headers?)
2) update canary workload to include a “repeatable prefix cohort” (say 20% of traffic) so hit-rate isn’t always ~0
3) dashboard panel: hit ratio + reused_tokens/sec next to p95 so oncall can correlate
4) no alerting on hit-rate initially; only include in incident-bot payload as context if delta > X

Rina Kobayashi: workload change is easy-ish. can do: keep prompts shape same but make the first N tokens identical for the cohort. we should confirm it doesn’t look like customer data (it won’t; it’s fixed synthetic string).

Omar El-Sayed: headers idea: runtime currently returns `x-rw-cache-hit` internally? not sure. i can add a boolean + reused_token_count to the internal debug stats payload that canary already records (without storing prompt).

Sofia Ivanova: if we do 20% repeated prefix, be careful: it can mask queueing regressions because cache hits reduce compute time -> shorter queues. canary could look “healthier” than real traffic if real traffic has fewer hits.

Noah Patel: good point. maybe we do two cohorts and keep them separated in metrics:
- cohort A: random (baseline raw)
- cohort B: repeat-prefix (cache-sensitive)
then we can see regressions that only show up when cache is involved.

Logan Wright: cohort label is fine if it’s `workload_profile="raw|cachey"` and only 2 values.

Nadia Rahman: if we add a cohort label, it doubles the time series. still ok but keep it limited. don’t add region+sku+model+cohort+cache_policy all at once if we can avoid it.

Omar El-Sayed: we can also make it “only emitted for top models” to keep series count down. canary config already has the model list.

Hiro Tanaka: for kernel attribution, cache stats help *indirectly*. if p95 regression happens w no cache hit change and runtime-only time increases, that’s on us. if cache hit ratio tanks and runtime-only time stays same, look at cache policy/evictions/fleet.

Sofia Ivanova: also can help explain tokens/sec drop that’s actually “more misses” not “slower decode”. we should show both.

Noah Patel: ok sounds like consensus is “yes, but careful”: counters, cohort separation, no alert gating.

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: make sure the metrics naming goes through the cardinality checklist. ping me/Nadia before merging.

Omar El-Sayed: i’ll draft a small PR in redwood runtime to emit:
- `prefix_cache_hit_total`
- `prefix_cache_miss_total`
- `prefix_cache_reused_tokens_total`
with labels: model, model_version, runtime_build, region, workload_profile (if passed), cache_policy.

Nadia Rahman: can we drop one dimension? do we need both cache_policy and workload_profile? maybe cache_policy is sufficient if workload profile is fixed.

Rina Kobayashi: workload profile isn’t fixed if we keep two cohorts. but we can encode the cohort in the canary-side metric (not runtime-side) to keep runtime metrics simpler.

Sofia Ivanova: +1. keep runtime emission minimal; canary can add the cohort dimension when it emits the aggregation.

Noah Patel: good compromise.

Noah Patel: Omar can you do runtime debug stats: `cache_hit` bool + `reused_tokens` count in the response telemetry payload, then canary emits the counters w workload_profile label.

Omar El-Sayed: yep, that’s cleaner. will do.

Hiro Tanaka: please add a note in the runbook: “cache hit-rate change can explain e2e p95 move; check hit ratio + queueing vs runtime-only.”

Noah Patel: will update runbook once we have the panel.

Logan Wright: also ensure we’re not leaking any prompt-derived hashes. only booleans/counts.

Rina Kobayashi: re: repeatable prefix string — i’ll use something like `"You are a helpful assistant. Answer concisely."` and keep it static.

Sofia Ivanova: add a check that the prefix cohort is small enough that it doesn’t distort overall p95. or plot cohort p95 separately.

Noah Patel: ack. we’ll keep overall alert signals based on raw cohort only. cachey cohort is for attribution panels.

Nadia Rahman: :thumbsup: that also reduces false positives from cache behavior changes.

Noah Patel: cool. action items:
- Omar: expose cache hit + reused tokens in internal stats payload; canary emits counters.
- Rina: add repeatable-prefix cohort option to prompt gen.
- Noah: add dashboard panel + add to incident-bot payload context (non-gating).

Noah Patel: thanks all.
text:
Noah Patel: quick q for perf-canary attribution: should we add a KV cache hit-rate signal to the canary metrics? thinking it might help explain p95 shifts where runtime-only looks flat but e2e latency moves (or vice versa).

Sofia Ivanova: do you mean KV cache like prefix/KV reuse across requests? (not the per-request kv cache that’s always there)

Noah Patel: yeah, the cross-request prefix cache / reuse. the one that gets better/worse depending on cache policy + eviction behavior.

Omar El-Sayed: technically we can expose it. runtime already knows when a request had a cache hit vs miss, plus how many tokens reused.

Hiro Tanaka: what’s the hypothesis? regressions we’ve seen from cache changes have been mostly memory pressure -> more evictions -> queueing/batching changes, not direct kernel perf.

Noah Patel: exactly. today canary screams “p95 up” and we’re guessing: scheduler? infra? cache? if we have a stable cache hit ratio + reused_tokens, we can say “hit-rate cratered” and stop blaming kernels.

Logan Wright: adding a metric is fine, but careful on labels/cardinality. do NOT do per-prefix or anything derived from prompt. should be aggregate counters/gauges.

Nadia Rahman: +1. also: what would “hit-rate” mean in canary? our current synthetic prompts are mostly random-ish shapes. we’ll get ~0% hits unless we intentionally repeat prefixes.

Rina Kobayashi: canary prompt generator right now does vary by bucket but we don’t repeat the same exact prefix often. we could add a “fixed system prefix” mode so we exercise the prefix cache similarly to prod (lots of apps have stable system prompt).

Sofia Ivanova: but do we *want* to exercise prefix cache? there’s a school of thought that canary should measure raw runtime throughput independent of caching. otherwise if cache hit rate goes up we get a “performance improvement” that isn’t from runtime.

Noah Patel: fair. i’m not proposing we gate on it (at least not v1). more like attribution-only panel + included in alert payload if it changed a lot.

Omar El-Sayed: implementation options:
- counter: `kv_prefix_cache_requests_total{hit="true|false", model=...}`
- counter: `kv_prefix_cache_reused_tokens_total{model=...}`
- gauge: `kv_prefix_cache_hit_ratio` computed in canary aggregator (avoid a new timeseries per label?)

Logan Wright: prefer counters + compute ratio in queries. gauges tend to flap if scrape aligns weird. counters w/ rate() are stable.

Nadia Rahman: also we should split “cache enabled” vs “cache disabled” explicitly. in some tiers it’s off.

Sofia Ivanova: +1. if canary runs against both hosted + dedicated w different cache policies, the hit-rate will be apples/oranges.

Noah Patel: can label `cache_policy` with low-card values? like `off|on_default|on_aggressive`.

Logan Wright: label cardinality ok if it’s a tiny enum controlled by config. make sure it’s validated (control plane) so someone can’t put arbitrary strings.

Hiro Tanaka: what’s the action item? i don’t want this to delay the current alert tuning.

Noah Patel: proposing:
1) add counters in runtime telemetry (or from canary side if we already get it in response headers?)
2) update canary workload to include a “repeatable prefix cohort” (say 20% of traffic) so hit-rate isn’t always ~0
3) dashboard panel: hit ratio + reused_tokens/sec next to p95 so oncall can correlate
4) no alerting on hit-rate initially; only include in incident-bot payload as context if delta > X

Rina Kobayashi: workload change is easy-ish. can do: keep prompts shape same but make the first N tokens identical for the cohort. we should confirm it doesn’t look like customer data (it won’t; it’s fixed synthetic string).

Omar El-Sayed: headers idea: runtime currently returns `x-rw-cache-hit` internally? not sure. i can add a boolean + reused_token_count to the internal debug stats payload that canary already records (without storing prompt).

Sofia Ivanova: if we do 20% repeated prefix, be careful: it can mask queueing regressions because cache hits reduce compute time -> shorter queues. canary could look “healthier” than real traffic if real traffic has fewer hits.

Noah Patel: good point. maybe we do two cohorts and keep them separated in metrics:
- cohort A: random (baseline raw)
- cohort B: repeat-prefix (cache-sensitive)
then we can see regressions that only show up when cache is involved.

Logan Wright: cohort label is fine if it’s `workload_profile="raw|cachey"` and only 2 values.

Nadia Rahman: if we add a cohort label, it doubles the time series. still ok but keep it limited. don’t add region+sku+model+cohort+cache_policy all at once if we can avoid it.

Omar El-Sayed: we can also make it “only emitted for top models” to keep series count down. canary config already has the model list.

Hiro Tanaka: for kernel attribution, cache stats help *indirectly*. if p95 regression happens w no cache hit change and runtime-only time increases, that’s on us. if cache hit ratio tanks and runtime-only time stays same, look at cache policy/evictions/fleet.

Sofia Ivanova: also can help explain tokens/sec drop that’s actually “more misses” not “slower decode”. we should show both.

Noah Patel: ok sounds like consensus is “yes, but careful”: counters, cohort separation, no alert gating.

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: make sure the metrics naming goes through the cardinality checklist. ping me/Nadia before merging.

Omar El-Sayed: i’ll draft a small PR in redwood runtime to emit:
- `prefix_cache_hit_total`
- `prefix_cache_miss_total`
- `prefix_cache_reused_tokens_total`
with labels: model, model_version, runtime_build, region, workload_profile (if passed), cache_policy.

Nadia Rahman: can we drop one dimension? do we need both cache_policy and workload_profile? maybe cache_policy is sufficient if workload profile is fixed.

Rina Kobayashi: workload profile isn’t fixed if we keep two cohorts. but we can encode the cohort in the canary-side metric (not runtime-side) to keep runtime metrics simpler.

Sofia Ivanova: +1. keep runtime emission minimal; canary can add the cohort dimension when it emits the aggregation.

Noah Patel: good compromise.

Noah Patel: Omar can you do runtime debug stats: `cache_hit` bool + `reused_tokens` count in the response telemetry payload, then canary emits the counters w workload_profile label.

Omar El-Sayed: yep, that’s cleaner. will do.

Hiro Tanaka: please add a note in the runbook: “cache hit-rate change can explain e2e p95 move; check hit ratio + queueing vs runtime-only.”

Noah Patel: will update runbook once we have the panel.

Logan Wright: also ensure we’re not leaking any prompt-derived hashes. only booleans/counts.

Rina Kobayashi: re: repeatable prefix string — i’ll use something like `"You are a helpful assistant. Answer concisely."` and keep it static.

Sofia Ivanova: add a check that the prefix cohort is small enough that it doesn’t distort overall p95. or plot cohort p95 separately.

Noah Patel: ack. we’ll keep overall alert signals based on raw cohort only. cachey cohort is for attribution panels.

Nadia Rahman: :thumbsup: that also reduces false positives from cache behavior changes.

Noah Patel: cool. action items:
- Omar: expose cache hit + reused tokens in internal stats payload; canary emits counters.
- Rina: add repeatable-prefix cohort option to prompt gen.
- Noah: add dashboard panel + add to incident-bot payload context (non-gating).

Noah Patel: thanks all.

title: #eng-runtime: 1769455201
preview:
Noah Patel: Want to lock this down today: what’s the *hard* GPU overhead budget for perf-canary per region + the QPS caps we’re enforcing? Also need a clear “disable switch” if we see any unexpected load.

Sofia Ivanova: For design doc we had <0.5% GPU capacity per region for covered models. Are we sticking to that or going more conservative for phase 1?

Sean Gallagher: +1 keep it conservative initially. SRE expectation: canary should be *invisible* to customer SLOs. If we get into a situation where canary contributes to queueing, we need a kill switch that doesn’t require waiting on deploys.

Vanessa Ortiz: From fleet side: us-east pool has some tighter headroom during peak. I’d prefer we budget against *peak* not average. 0.5% is ok if it’s real cap, not “best effort”.

Noah Patel: Ok, proposal:
- Phase 1 (us-east, 2-3 models): target 0.25% GPU overhead, hard cap 0.5%
- Phase 2+ (more models/regions): target 0.5%, hard cap 0.75% only with SRE signoff
Thoughts?

Omar El-Sayed: Works for me, but we need to define what “GPU overhead” means in code. Right now PR-19231 budgeter is based on request rate + avg tokens. Not actual SM utilization.

Logan Wright: Telemetry can give you per-request runtime_ms and tokens_out, so you can approximate “GPU-seconds” consumed by canary (per model/SKU). Not perfect but good enough to enforce caps.

Nadia Rahman: We can also emit a canary-only counter for “budget_exceeded_drops” so we can see if we’re constantly throttling.

Sofia Ivanova: Re: definition: easiest is cap based on *canary tokens/sec* relative to observed production tokens/sec for that model+region (from metrics). But that’s circular if prod changes.

Noah Patel: Yeah don’t want coupling to prod traffic volume. Prefer absolute ceilings that are safe regardless of prod.

Sean Gallagher: Agreed. Treat it like a load test running in prod: absolute limit.

Rina Kobayashi: Can we cap by “inflight requests” too? Some of the longer prompts will hang around and mess with batching if we let it get spiky.

Omar El-Sayed: We already have per-target `max_concurrency` in the service skeleton. We can set it to 1-2 per model per region for phase 1.

Noah Patel: Let’s nail numbers. For a single model target in a region:
- max_concurrency: 2
- max_qps: 0.05 (1 req / 20s) ?
- max_tokens_out: 256 (for most shapes) with occasional 512 bucket
This yields ~0.1 concurrent steady state and minimal impact.

Sofia Ivanova: 0.05 qps feels *too* low for p95 stability. You’ll need enough samples to be confident. What’s our min sample size window for regression eval again?

Nadia Rahman: In the evaluator we were discussing min 200 samples per metric window (and also min 30 mins). At 0.05 qps you get 180/hr, so you’re borderline.

Noah Patel: Good catch.

Rina Kobayashi: Also prompts are mixed buckets; if only 180/hr, each bucket is tiny. Can we do 0.2 qps per model (1 req / 5s) and keep concurrency 2?

Vanessa Ortiz: If it’s 0.2 qps *per model* and we do 3 models, that’s 0.6 qps total. Still small. I’m more worried about worst-case long sequences.

Hiro Tanaka: Kernel-wise, we’ll catch regressions faster if we keep decode lengths representative. But we don’t need huge QPS; we need consistent signal. I’m okay with 0.2 qps if we cap max tokens and enforce strict backoff.

Omar El-Sayed: Implementation detail: we can enforce two limits:
1) `qps_limit` token bucket (global per region + per model)
2) `gpu_time_budget_ms_per_minute` using runtime_ms from responses (or estimated if missing)
If budget trips, we skip the run and log `budget_blocked=1`.

Logan Wright: Please be careful with labels on the budget metrics. If you slice by prompt_shape_id or seed you’ll blow up cardinality. Keep it to model, region, sku, runtime_version.

Noah Patel: Yep.

Sean Gallagher: Kill switch: what’s the mechanism?
- control plane flag (preferred)
- helm value + redeploy (too slow)

Omar El-Sayed: Control plane already has a config surface for “canary target models per region”. We can add `enabled=false` at the top-level and have the agent poll every 30s.

Sofia Ivanova: 30s polling is fine. Also can have a local env var override so oncall can `kubectl set env` as emergency.

Vanessa Ortiz: + please add a “safe-disable” that doesn’t cause retries/backpressure on inference endpoints. Like: if disabled, stop sending requests entirely, don’t keep connections open.

Omar El-Sayed: Yep. When disabled: cancel the scheduler loop, drain inflight (don’t start new), close clients.

Nadia Rahman: For alerting: we should page if canary overhead budget is exceeded? Or warn only?

Sean Gallagher: Warn only. Paging should be reserved for performance regressions on top models. Overhead is an operational hygiene metric; if it’s exceeded, oncall can just disable canary and follow up next business day.

Noah Patel: Ok, writing down decisions:
- Phase 1 target overhead: 0.25% GPU per region; hard cap 0.5% (enforced by budget logic)
- Per model defaults: max_qps=0.2, max_concurrency=2
- Global region cap too (sum across models) so config mistakes can’t fan out
- Control plane kill switch: `perf_canary.enabled=false` + polling every 30s
- If cap exceeded: canary drops/skips runs; emits `perf_canary_budget_blocked_total` and logs once/min

Sofia Ivanova: Can we also add a “panic disable” in runtime control plane UI for SRE? Like a single toggle without PRs.

Noah Patel: That’s in Connor’s rollout checklist stuff but we can piggyback. For now: config change in control plane with audit log.

Logan Wright: If you want to quantify “<0.5% GPU” you’ll need a baseline reference. For phase 1 we can just compute:
`canary_runtime_ms_sum / (num_gpus * 60_000ms)` over 5 min windows.
Not perfect but gives a hard upper bound assuming runtime_ms ~ gpu-time.

Hiro Tanaka: Good enough. It’ll at least catch if canary is accidentally spamming or getting stuck in retries.

Rina Kobayashi: What about retries? If inference returns 5xx, canary currently retries 3x (I think). That can blow budget during an incident.

Omar El-Sayed: Great point. We should disable retries by default, or max 1 retry with jitter, and retries count towards budget.

Sean Gallagher: Please default to no retries. During an outage we want less traffic, not more.

Noah Patel: +1. Omar can you update config: `retries=0` in prod values.

Omar El-Sayed: Will do.

Vanessa Ortiz: Also: if we detect queueing time spike attributed to canary, do we auto-disable?

Sofia Ivanova: That’s tricky; queueing isn’t necessarily canary-caused. I’d avoid auto-disable logic based on queueing signal.

Sean Gallagher: Agree. Keep auto-disable only on our own internal counters (budget exceeded) and explicit manual toggle.

Noah Patel: Cool. Action items:
- Omar: implement region+model token bucket + runtime_ms budget; add control plane `enabled` flag; set retries=0 (ETA EOD?)
- Nadia/Logan: confirm metric names + labels for budget blocked and canary request totals
- Vanessa: sanity-check phase 1 numbers vs us-east peak headroom

Omar El-Sayed: ack, I’ll push an update to PR-19231 + follow-up for control plane toggle.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: once this is in, SRE is comfortable with phase 1 enablement.

Sofia Ivanova: :thumbsup: please make sure the dashboard has a panel for “canary QPS / blocked by budget” so we can see if we’re under-sampling.

Noah Patel: will add to dashboard spec thread as well. Thanks all.

title: #eng-runtime: 1769202109
preview:
Sofia Ivanova: quick sanity check request — can someone help validate the scheduler queueing-time metric under load? I’m seeing queue_time_p95 jump but E2E p95 is flat, which feels… off.

Omar El-Sayed: which metric + labels are you looking at?

Sofia Ivanova: `runtime.scheduler.queue_time_ms` (histogram) split by model + region. Dashboard panel in the new canary view is “Queueing (p95)”.

Noah Patel: is that from canary traffic only or all traffic?

Sofia Ivanova: canary only. label `source=perf-canary`.

Logan Wright: fwiw we changed the instrumentation boundary in the last runtime PR — we should verify definition is still “enqueue -> begin_execution” and not “enqueue -> first_token”.

Omar El-Sayed: yeah definition should be:
- start: request accepted by scheduler and placed into pending queue
- end: batch is formed and request is handed to execution (prefill)
NOT including GPU compute.

Caleb Johnson: can you paste the query / panel link? (I can check units + hist bucket config)

Sofia Ivanova: query from panel:
```
histogram_quantile(0.95,
  sum(rate(runtime_scheduler_queue_time_ms_bucket{source="perf-canary", model=~"$model", region=~"$region"}[5m])) by (le)
)
```

Omar El-Sayed: ok that looks right in principle.

Noah Patel: what’s the canary load shape when it spikes? does it correlate with batch size changes?

Sofia Ivanova: that’s the weird part — batch size panel is stable-ish. `batch_size_p50 ~ 6`, `p95 ~ 10`.

Vanessa Ortiz: any fleet events in that window? autoscaler moving pods / GPU drain?

Sofia Ivanova: not that I can tell. us-east, A100 pool.

Logan Wright: possibility: we’re double-counting time if a request is re-queued (e.g., prefill admission fails / retry path). Under high load, that might be frequent.

Omar El-Sayed: there *is* a “reinsert” path when we miss batch cutoffs. But queue_time should be measured per attempt or per request? Currently per request attempt, I think.

Noah Patel: for dashboards/alerts we want per request *total* queueing before execution, not per attempt. Otherwise p95 will balloon without affecting E2E much if attempts are fast? hm.

Caleb Johnson: another angle: units. metric says `_ms` but is it actually emitting seconds? I’ve been burned.

Omar El-Sayed: it’s emitted via `observe(duration_ms)` in the runtime, should be ms.

Caleb Johnson: ok. but panel label says “ms” and axis is seconds in Grafana? sometimes it auto-scales.

Sofia Ivanova: axis is ms and values are like 40–60ms baseline, spike to 220–300ms p95.

Noah Patel: can we reproduce with a controlled load run? If we run perf-canary locally against staging at 2–3x QPS, does queue_time scale linearly with concurrency?

Sofia Ivanova: I ran the load generator in staging and queue_time stayed ~0 even when E2E went up. That also feels wrong.

Omar El-Sayed: staging has fewer concurrent tenants; scheduler queue might not engage the same way. But “~0” suggests we might be ending the timer too early.

Logan Wright: can we confirm the “end” event is firing when request is *dequeued* vs when it is *enqueued into batch candidate list*? There’s a subtle gap between “picked as candidate” and “actually executed” if the batch waits for more tokens.

Omar El-Sayed: the code path is `Scheduler::pop_ready()` -> returns `WorkItem` -> enqueue to executor. The timer ends at `pop_ready()`. If pop_ready returns early and then we wait on batch fill, that waiting time is not counted.

Noah Patel: that would explain staging (queue is empty, pop_ready immediate => 0ms), but not prod spikes (why would pop_ready wait?). Unless the queue mutex contention is being counted? hmm.

Caleb Johnson: wait, histogram is `runtime_scheduler_queue_time_ms`. If that includes lock wait, under load mutex contention could show up as “queue time” even if real queue depth isn’t high.

Omar El-Sayed: we measure from `enqueue_ts` stored on the request object. Not lock wait.

Sofia Ivanova: I can add a temporary debug panel: queue depth gauge vs queue time. Do we have queue depth metric?

Omar El-Sayed: we have `runtime.scheduler.pending_requests` gauge in newer builds, but I’m not sure it’s on in prod yet.

Logan Wright: it’s behind `telemetry.scheduler_depth=true` flag; I can check if it’s enabled for canary pools.

Grafana Bot: Panel link shared by Sofia Ivanova: https://grafana.redwood.example/d/perf-canary-model?viewPanel=23 (Queueing p95)

Nadia Rahman: looking at the raw time series: the spike coincides with a drop in `runtime.executor.tokens_per_sec` for ~3 minutes, then both recover. So maybe it’s real queueing due to slower execute.

Sofia Ivanova: ok that’s helpful. but e2e p95 not moving is still confusing.

Nadia Rahman: e2e p95 panel is using a different window (15m) and it’s smoothed; queue panel is 5m. If you zoom, e2e has a small bump (~+6-8%), just not obvious.

Noah Patel: ah. we should align the dashboard windows to avoid misleading comparisons.

Caleb Johnson: +1. also, queue_time is runtime-only; e2e includes network + gateway. if runtime regresses but gateway cache hits also change, can cancel.

Sofia Ivanova: ok. but we still need to verify queue_time math is correct.

Omar El-Sayed: I can do a quick “instrumentation truth test”: add a span event in tracing for enqueue/dequeue timestamps and compare.

Logan Wright: please do it in staging first; prod traces for canary are ok but keep cardinality low.

Noah Patel: can we define an expected invariant?
- `e2e_latency_ms` >= `queue_time_ms + execution_time_ms` (within some epsilon)
If queue_time spikes but execution_time + queue_time doesn’t, it’s broken.

Sofia Ivanova: we have execution time?

Omar El-Sayed: we have `runtime.request.compute_time_ms` for prefill+decode combined, but it’s per batch item and might exclude some CPU work.

Caleb Johnson: for a spot-check, pick one model + region and compare p95s with same window:
```
Q = p95(queue)
C = p95(compute)
E = p95(e2e)
Expect: E ~ Q + C + overhead
```

Nadia Rahman: I ran that for `llama-3.1-70b` us-east canary:
- Q p95 spiked to ~280ms
- C p95 spiked to ~1.9s (normally ~1.5s)
- E p95 spiked to ~2.35s (normally ~2.0s)
So it does add up.

Sofia Ivanova: ok so metric likely fine; dashboard just made it look inconsistent.

Noah Patel: action items then:
1) update dashboard: force same time window + show a stacked panel (queue vs compute vs other)
2) add queue depth metric panel where available
3) document definition in the panel description / runbook

Omar El-Sayed: I’ll also confirm we’re not double-counting on retry/requeue. Will audit code path and add a `requeue_count` counter.

Logan Wright: +1 on `requeue_count` — that’s a great attribution signal (scheduler heuristic changes tend to increase it).

Vanessa Ortiz: if we see Q and C spike together again, I’ll check fleet events and GPU clock throttling. ping me in-thread.

Sofia Ivanova: thanks all. I’ll file a quick dashboard tweak ticket and link it here.

Sofia Ivanova: (reactions: :thumbsup: x4)

Caleb Johnson: one more nit: current panel groups only by `le` (no model). if someone adds `$model=all`, it’ll blend histograms across models and get nonsense p95. Can we lock it to single model or aggregate by (le, model) and then select model?

Nadia Rahman: +1 please. we had that exact issue in the alert storm postmortem.

Sofia Ivanova: good catch. will update query to `... by (le, model)` and require model filter.

Logan Wright: cool. once that lands, we should mirror the query shape in alert rules so we don’t regress later.

Noah Patel: marking this as “metric looks correct; dashboard/query needs adjustments + add supporting signals.”

Sofia Ivanova: `runtime.scheduler.queue_time_ms` (histogram) split by model + region. Dashboard panel in the new canary view is “Queueing (p95)”.

Noah Patel: is that from canary traffic only or all traffic?

Sofia Ivanova: canary only. label `source=perf-canary`.

Logan Wright: fwiw we changed the instrumentation boundary in the last runtime PR — we should verify definition is still “enqueue -> begin_execution” and not “enqueue -> first_token”.

Omar El-Sayed: yeah definition should be:
- start: request accepted by scheduler and placed into pending queue
- end: batch is formed and request is handed to execution (prefill)
NOT including GPU compute.

Caleb Johnson: can you paste the query / panel link? (I can check units + hist bucket config)

Sofia Ivanova: query from panel:
```
histogram_quantile(0.95,
  sum(rate(runtime_scheduler_queue_time_ms_bucket{source="perf-canary", model=~"$model", region=~"$region"}[5m])) by (le)
)
```

title: #eng-runtime: 1769007005
preview:
Omar El-Sayed: heads up: I think we have a bug in the runtime export for prefill/decode token split. Seeing `prefill_tokens` ~= `total_tokens` on a bunch of streaming requests, which is impossible unless decode=0.

Sofia Ivanova: where are you seeing it? grafana or raw events?

Omar El-Sayed: raw telemetry rows (stage breakdown). Example request_id `req_8f2c...`:
- total_tokens=842
- prefill_tokens=842
- decode_tokens=811
So prefill+decode > total. That’s what’s feeding cost-model breakdown.

Grace Kim: oof. that looks like we’re emitting per-stage counters as cumulative instead of delta.

Logan Wright: +1, we saw a sudden jump in `rw.runtime.tokens.prefill` at ~02/18 21:00 UTC when the new exporter started rolling. Drift SLI got noisy but totals still kinda matched because billing is using total_tokens.

Nadia Rahman: confirming: the canonical token meter has total prompt+completion correct. The stage split is used for cost attribution (prefill vs decode pricing curve) + cache modeling. If stage split is wrong, Console breakdown will look wrong even if invoices match.

Ethan Park: is this tied to PR-28509 (runtime export KV cache stats + prefill/decode)?

Omar El-Sayed: yeah, I bisected to that rollout. In the exporter we do:
```cpp
// pseudo
prefill_tokens += batch.prefill_tokens;
decode_tokens  += batch.decode_tokens;
emit(prefill_tokens, decode_tokens);
```
The consumer expects per-interval counts, not cumulative. We should emit delta per flush or reset after emit.

Sofia Ivanova: wait, are we using stats object that already accumulates per-request? if we emit multiple times (streaming), it’ll keep adding.

Grace Kim: I looked at the code quickly: `RequestStats` holds cumulative counts since request start, but exporter runs on every `on_token()` tick and sends whatever is in the struct. So we’re effectively sending cumulative snapshots and telemetry is summing them. :facepalm:

Thomas Baird: that explains the cardinality + why p95 prefill tokens exploded. Aggregator is SUM() not MAX().

Benji Okafor: can telemetry change aggregation to use MAX for those fields?

Logan Wright: we could, but it’s a contract change and risky. Also for non-streaming we only emit once so SUM==MAX. For streaming we’d need request_id-level rollup with MAX; not currently safe in all pipelines.

Ethan Park: preferred fix is runtime exporter sends deltas (or emits only once at end). Console hot path wants per-request breakdown; we should keep semantics consistent with schema.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. Fix options:
A) Emit only once at request end (needs reliable end hook; we have one)
B) Emit deltas per flush (`delta = current - last_emitted`)
C) Keep emitting cumulative but add `aggregation_hint=max` (too late)
I vote B so streaming visibility still works if anyone is looking, but downstream can safely SUM.

Sofia Ivanova: B seems right. We already track `last_emitted_prefill/decode` for latency? If not, easy to add.

Grace Kim: note: decode_tokens can move even after prefill is done; prefill should stop once the prompt is processed. So deltas are straightforward.

Nadia Rahman: does this also affect cache-hit tokens? We’re using `rw.runtime.kvcache.hit_tokens` to compute effective prefill compute.

Omar El-Sayed: yes, same pattern. `cache_hit_tokens` is cumulative too. So it’s being overcounted on streaming requests.

Logan Wright: impact: cost model breakdown for affected window will show nonsense (prefill > total, cache hit rate > 1, etc.). But billing totals should be ok.

Ethan Park: we need two things:
1) patch in runtime ASAP + validate in staging
2) backfill / correction plan for the window so Console/Optimize charts aren’t misleading

Thomas Baird: backfill options:
- If we still have per-request snapshots, we can recompute as MAX(prefill_tokens) over request_id instead of SUM.
- If only aggregated per-minute sums exist, can’t fully reconstruct.
What do we persist at the finest grain?

Logan Wright: we have request_id-level events in the telemetry store for 14 days. After that it’s rolled up. So we can backfill last ~2 weeks.

Sofia Ivanova: how far back does the buggy exporter go?

Omar El-Sayed: rollout started Feb 18. So we’re within 14d retention. Good.

Grace Kim: patch ETA: I can get a diff up in ~1h. Need Omar/Sofia to review.

Omar El-Sayed: cool. Please include a unit test that simulates 3 emits for a streaming request and asserts downstream-summed == final counts.

Benji Okafor: for backfill, do we run a one-off job in telemetry pipeline or patch in cost-model service to use MAX when it sees schema_version X?

Ethan Park: I’d rather backfill telemetry + also add a guardrail in cost-model service: if `prefill+decode > total * 1.05` then mark breakdown as `degraded` and fall back to total-only attribution (no stage split) for that request. We can remove later.

Nadia Rahman: +1 on guardrail. Console can show a tooltip “breakdown unavailable” instead of wrong numbers.

Logan Wright: I can take the backfill: write a replay that recomputes per-request stage totals as MAX over request_id for the affected metric set:
- `rw.runtime.tokens.prefill`
- `rw.runtime.tokens.decode`
- `rw.runtime.kvcache.hit_tokens`
…and re-materialize the derived table cost-model reads.

Thomas Baird: please ensure the recompute uses the event-time watermark correctly; otherwise we’ll skew.

Sofia Ivanova: also watch for requests that legitimately emit multiple segments across reschedules. MAX should still work since counters are cumulative within request.

Grace Kim: posting patch branch soon. Also: should we bump exporter semantic version? It’s technically a bugfix but changes observed deltas.

Ethan Park: bump patch version in exporter metadata so we can track adoption (`runtime_exporter_version`). Not a schema major.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. And please add a debug log for negative deltas (should never happen unless stats reset mid-request).

Grace Kim: ok. draft PR will include:
- track `last_emitted_*` per request
- emit deltas
- reset last_emitted on request end
- negative delta clamp + log

Logan Wright: for comms: I’ll ping Console folks that breakdowns for 2/18-2/?? might be weird until backfill lands. We can point to drift dashboard.

Nadia Rahman: I’ll update the cost-model validation harness to add an invariant: `prefill_tokens + decode_tokens <= total_tokens + epsilon` and `0 <= cache_hit_tokens <= prefill_tokens + epsilon`. This would have caught it.

Sofia Ivanova: :thumbsup: also let’s add a canary alert on that invariant in prod.

Omar El-Sayed: agreed. Once PR is up, tag me + Sofia.

Grace Kim: PR is up (runtime): “Fix streaming prefill/decode token export to emit deltas” (link). Reviews appreciated.

Omar El-Sayed: looking now.

Omar El-Sayed: review: looks good. One nit: use int64 for the counters; some big batch jobs can exceed 2^31 tokens across long-lived streams.

Grace Kim: good catch, updated.

Logan Wright: I’ll start the backfill job after patch deploys (so we don’t mix semantics mid-window). Target: run overnight, verify by comparing distribution of (prefill+decode)/total.

Ethan Park: action items recap:
- Grace: land runtime patch today, coordinate deploy
- Logan: backfill request-level stage totals for 2/18-present; publish note in #eng-platform/#console
- Nadia: add invariants to validation harness + alert proposal
- Ethan: add temporary cost-model guardrail fallback

Sofia Ivanova: thanks all. please also add a short postmortem note somewhere (even lightweight) so we remember why the backfill happened.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. I’ll write a quick internal note once deploy is confirmed.

title: #eng-runtime: 1768793205
preview:
Sofia Ivanova: ok folks, final review thread for batching tier defaults before we cut runtime-1.22. Please sanity check the knob sets + call out remaining known issues we should document.

Sofia Ivanova: context: we reconciled the fast-tier p99 regression from last week + merged the cardinality hotfix. now we need to lock:
- defaults per tier (fast / standard / cost-efficient)
- any model-family constraints (70B vs 8B)
- overload behavior + guardrails

Sofia Ivanova: proposed FINAL values (decoder-only families; applies to Llama3.x, Mistral, Qwen2 unless noted)

FAST
- max_wait_ms: 2
- max_batch_tokens_total: 2048
- max_batch_requests: 8
- prefill_concurrency_limit: 2
- decode_step_tokens_cap: 256  (cap per tick to reduce long-decode hogging)
- admission_control: on
  - queue_soft_limit: 24
  - queue_hard_limit: 40 (reject/429)
- anti_starvation: on (age-based bump after 80ms)

STANDARD
- max_wait_ms: 6
- max_batch_tokens_total: 4096
- max_batch_requests: 16
- prefill_concurrency_limit: 3
- decode_step_tokens_cap: 512
- admission_control: on
  - queue_soft_limit: 64
  - queue_hard_limit: 120
- anti_starvation: on (age-based bump after 150ms)

COST-EFFICIENT
- max_wait_ms: 12
- max_batch_tokens_total: 8192
- max_batch_requests: 32
- prefill_concurrency_limit: 4
- decode_step_tokens_cap: 1024
- admission_control: on
  - queue_soft_limit: 128
  - queue_hard_limit: 240
- anti_starvation: on (age-based bump after 250ms)

Sofia Ivanova: 70B note: cap max_batch_tokens_total to 6144 for cost-efficient on H100-80G due to observed prefill spikes + KV pressure; on A100-80G cap to 4096.

Sofia Ivanova: streaming note: if request.streaming=true, enforce max_wait_ms = min(max_wait_ms, 4) for standard/cost-efficient. fast unchanged.

Sofia Ivanova: reason codes we will emit (per PR-28473):
- tier_default
- route_override
- tenant_override
- model_constraint
- overload_mode

Sofia Ivanova: remaining known issues I think we should carry:
1) mixed extremely-long prompts + short streaming in same queue: occasional jitter (still within p95 for standard, but p99 in fast can spike if we don’t shed)
2) very bursty traffic on cost-efficient: queue depth can oscillate; mitigated by hard limits but we should document it

Sofia Ivanova: pls reply with any “blocker” or “write it down as known limitation” by EOD.

Lena Fischer: :+1: these match what I have in the candidate sheet after the last rerun.

Lena Fischer: extra detail from the last benchmark pass (mixed workloads scenario v3):
- FAST: p99 improved after we lowered max_batch_tokens_total from 3072 -> 2048 + added decode_step_tokens_cap=256.
- STANDARD: stable; biggest win was prefill_concurrency_limit=3 (kept prefill from starving decode)
- COST-EFFICIENT: gains plateau after 8192 tokens; beyond that we got tail blowups under concurrency ramp

Lena Fischer: I’d like us to explicitly document the “streaming clamp” (min wait 4ms) in release notes. Otherwise folks will compare max_wait_ms=12 and assume we’re buffering streaming tokens for 12ms, which we aren’t.

Marcus Reed: perf suite results summary (topline; H100 hosted cluster, llama3.1-8b + llama3.1-70b):
- FAST tier: meets p95 target across both; p99 for 70b is still tight but within gate after admission control is enabled.
- STANDARD: meets p95 and p99 gates with margin
- COST-EFFICIENT: meets p95; p99 occasionally crosses gate on 70b when we allow >6k batch tokens

Marcus Reed: so I’m aligned w/ the 70B caps Sofia called out.

Marcus Reed: I also want to note: “max_batch_requests” matters more than we thought for short prompts. If we leave requests uncapped, batcher packs too many small reqs and decode gets spiky. The 8/16/32 caps fixed that without costing much throughput.

Omar El-Sayed: on fast tier: I’m good with max_wait_ms=2 + tight token caps.

Omar El-Sayed: one nuance: decode_step_tokens_cap=256 is doing a lot of work for tail. but it can reduce throughput for long outputs (obviously) and cost-efficient customers will complain if they accidentally land in fast.

Omar El-Sayed: so I’d like to ensure:
- default tier selection stays STANDARD unless explicitly configured
- overload_mode behavior for FAST is “shed earlier” not “increase batching”

Omar El-Sayed: known issue to add: if a tenant sets FAST on a route that routinely emits 2k+ output tokens, they’ll see throughput cliffs. that’s expected but we should state it.

Julian Moore: +1 on documenting overload behavior.

Julian Moore: current logic in PR-28480 branch is:
- FAST: overload_mode triggers at queue_soft_limit; actions: (1) turn on strict admission, (2) start returning 429 for new reqs once hard limit hit. we DO NOT increase wait.
- STANDARD/COST-EFFICIENT: overload_mode triggers at 1.2x queue_soft_limit; actions: (1) clamp max_batch_tokens_total down one “step” (e.g., 8192->6144) to reduce queue growth, (2) clamp max_wait_ms to 8 in cost-efficient.

Julian Moore: the second point might look counterintuitive (“cost-efficient clamps wait down”), but it prevents queue runaway and improves overall completion rate. we should mention it somewhere or we’ll get questions.

Sean Gallagher: SRE take: defaults look sane.

Sean Gallagher: for fast tier p99: please confirm the gating we’re using in canary is *per-region* not global aggregated. last time we got burned by a noisy region hiding in the mean.

Marcus Reed: yep, per-region gates are on in perf-canary PR-489. failures trigger on worst region, not avg.

Sean Gallagher: great.

Sean Gallagher: known issues list: can we add a bullet about “under overload, fast-tier will shed; customers may see more 429s rather than latency inflation”? That’s the right behavior but support will need to know.

Allison Grant: observability note: with tier labels re-enabled post-hotfix (pr-28544), we’re safe on cardinality if:
- tier is only one of {fast, standard, cost_efficient}
- we do NOT tag reason_code as a metric label on high-QPS histograms (it remains an event + sampled trace attribute)

Allison Grant: please don’t let anyone add `reason_code` to `latency_ms_bucket{...}` later “for convenience”. :)

Lena Fischer: re model-family constraints: we should call out qwen2-72b behaves like llama70b (same caps). mistral-large is even more sensitive (prefill spikes). I’d propose: any model with hidden_size>=8192 gets the 70B caps by default.

Sofia Ivanova: agree. we can implement that in the catalog constraint mapping (Quentin already has the `batching_profile_class` field). for the doc, we’ll phrase it as “large models may apply tighter batch-token caps automatically.”

Rina Kobayashi: benchmark lab note: the published profiles should include both “base” and “large-model constrained” variants. Otherwise external folks will try to reproduce with 70B and wonder why their numbers don’t match.

Rina Kobayashi: I can update the profile publication page to show:
- fast/base
- standard/base
- cost-efficient/base
- cost-efficient/large-model-cap
and footnote the auto-constraint.

Jonas Weber: evals/quality: no regressions correlated with these batching changes in the promptset runs. biggest “difference” reports we saw were actually truncation from customer max_tokens settings.

Jonas Weber: but I want one known issue: cost-efficient + aggressive batching can increase time-to-first-token for streaming when customers send long prompts. the “streaming clamp” helps, but TTFT still shifts upward vs fast. expected tradeoff; we should be explicit.

Omar El-Sayed: +1; TTFT vs throughput tradeoff is literally the whole tier story.

Marcus Reed: small clarification: streaming clamp currently is `if streaming then max_wait_ms = min(max_wait_ms, 4)` for standard and cost-efficient. In practice, fast already at 2.

Marcus Reed: with that, streaming TTFT delta for cost-efficient was ~+35-60ms p50 vs standard in the mixed scenario. (still within standard tier target for TTFT, but customers expecting “instant” should use fast.)

Sofia Ivanova: capturing known issues / limitations to include in release notes + docs:
- FAST is not intended for long generations; throughput cliffs are expected for high output-token routes
- Under overload, FAST sheds (429) rather than inflating latency
- For large models (hidden_size>=8192), cost-efficient may apply tighter max_batch_tokens_total caps automatically
- Streaming TTFT is higher in cost-efficient vs standard/fast; clamp prevents worst-case buffering but can’t remove tradeoff
- Mixed extreme-long prefill + short streaming can still create jitter; mitigated by anti-starvation + decode cap

Sofia Ivanova: anything else before we lock?

Allison Grant: last ask: ensure the dashboard panels show effective tier + whether large-model constraint was applied. otherwise debugging becomes impossible.

Julian Moore: that’s in the reason codes: `model_constraint` emitted when large-model cap triggers. Console can show “Effective profile: cost-efficient (large-model cap)” with that.

Sean Gallagher: from SRE, this is good to ship.

Mateo Alvarez: I’m good with these final defaults.

Mateo Alvarez: Sofia pls make sure the release notes call out the overload behavior + the fact we’re defaulting to STANDARD unless the route policy opts into fast/cost-efficient. That’s the main expectation-setting.

Mateo Alvarez: once that’s in, consider this sign-off for runtime-1.22 cut. :thumbsup:

Sofia Ivanova: ack. locking values as proposed. I’ll update the release notes doc + ping Connor for train inclusion. thanks all.

title: #eng-runtime: 1768690388
preview:
Hiro Tanaka: we need better attribution on perf regressions. can we tag *which* kernel got picked + runtime build id in telemetry? right now canary says “p95 up 18%” and we spend 2h guessing if it’s scheduler vs kernel vs fleet.

Noah Patel: +1. for perf-canary we can include these in the alert payload too (so incident-bot shows it).

Omar El-Sayed: runtime already knows kernel selection internally, but we don’t export it today. question is what granularity we can expose without blowing up cardinality.

Logan Wright: ^ yep. if we put full kernel name per request as a label, prometheus is gonna have a bad time. we should aim for stable, low-cardinality tags.

Sofia Ivanova: what do we actually need to answer quickly?
- did kernel implementation change (new fused attention variant, etc)
- did selection policy change (picked different kernel for same shape)
- did runtime build / flags change

Hiro Tanaka: exactly. ideally:
1) runtime build id (git sha or build number)
2) kernel “family” / “impl id” (stable string)
3) kernel selection version (policy version)
optional: “reason” or “selector path” (e.g. `flash_v2` vs `triton_fallback`) but can be maybe just a small enum.

Nadia Rahman: for baselines we’re keying on model+runtime_version+sku(+region). if we add kernel id, do we want baseline split by it? or just use it for triage only.

Noah Patel: triage-only for now. baseline split would fragment samples.

Caleb Johnson: instrumentation-wise: we can put build+kernel tags into traces (span attrs) and then have metrics attach a coarser label set. i’m thinking:
- traces: `runtime.build_sha`, `runtime.kernel.impl`, `runtime.kernel.selector_ver`
- metrics: maybe only `runtime.build_id` and `runtime.kernel.impl_bucket`

Logan Wright: please do not put `build_sha` as a metric label if it changes every deploy. that’s high churn / can cause series churn. we have `runtime_release` already (e.g. `2026.01.18-rc3`). can we use that?

Omar El-Sayed: we can emit a stable release id string (the one that’s in control plane) instead of raw sha. but the kernel impl can also change via feature flag without a release bump.

Vanessa Ortiz: fleet changes also matter. we should make sure canary dashboards already show `gpu_sku` and `driver_version`. tagging kernel will help narrow to runtime vs infra.

Hiro Tanaka: kernel changes behind flags: can we export a “kernel bundle version” that increments when we change kernel binaries / configs? even if runtime release stays.

Omar El-Sayed: possible. there’s already a kernel registry artifact version used by the build pipeline. we can surface that as `kernel_bundle_id`.

Logan Wright: ok `kernel_bundle_id` as a metric label is probably fine if it changes less frequently than build sha. we still need to keep the value space small.

Sofia Ivanova: what about selection? i want to know if scheduler changes caused different batch shapes which caused selector to pick a different kernel. if we have `selector_ver` + `impl`, we can at least see “impl switched”.

Noah Patel: can we also log when impl switches for the same model/version/sku? like a counter: `kernel_selection_change_total`?

Omar El-Sayed: careful: “same shape” definition is tricky. but we can do a coarse counter in canary itself: during a run, track which impl ids observed per model and include it in the summary.

Caleb Johnson: metrics proposal (names tbd):
- `inference_runtime_request_latency_ms` (hist)
labels: `model`, `model_version`, `gpu_sku`, `region`, `runtime_release`, `kernel_bundle_id`, `kernel_impl_group`
where `kernel_impl_group` is a controlled enum like:
- `flashattn_v2`
- `flashattn_v3`
- `triton_sdpa`
- `cutlass_sdpa`
- `fallback_eager`
- `unknown`

Logan Wright: that label set might be ok if `kernel_impl_group` stays tiny. please do not add raw kernel name (`flash_v3_sm90_s128_kv8...`). also: do we really need `model_version` on *all* runtime metrics? we already agreed in cardinality review to prefer canonical model id + version only on canary-emitted metrics, not all prod traffic.

Omar El-Sayed: this is for canary traffic only? (perf-canary service account)

Noah Patel: yes, canary traffic + maybe a small sample on internal benchmark jobs. not general customer traffic.

Logan Wright: perfect. then cardinality is bounded by config (top models, a few regions). i’m good with `model_version` there.

Hiro Tanaka: can we also ensure alert payload includes these fields so we don’t have to click 3 dashboards?

Noah Patel: yep. incident-bot handler can show:
- model, region, sku
- runtime_release (observed)
- kernel_bundle_id + kernel_impl_group (mode over last N samples)
- baseline runtime_release + baseline kernel_bundle_id (if different)

Nadia Rahman: baseline store currently stores `runtime_release` and `gpu_sku` already. we can add optional fields `kernel_bundle_id` and `kernel_impl_group_mode`. as “metadata” only.

Sofia Ivanova: question: if kernel impl differs between baseline and observed, do we treat as regression automatically?

Hiro Tanaka: not automatically. but it’s a “big hint”. if selector changed, we should know immediately.

Omar El-Sayed: implementation detail: kernel selection happens in the attention op. we can plumb a thread-local “kernel id” that gets attached to the request context at end of request.

Caleb Johnson: if it’s thread-local, we need to be careful under async + batching. better: attach to request struct / trace context explicitly.

Omar El-Sayed: good point. in batching we process multiple requests in one kernel call, but can still attribute the impl per batch. probably record kernel tags per-batch and attach to each request span.

Logan Wright: for metrics, per-request labels are ok in canary. but please ensure we don’t accidentally enable this for all traffic later. guard behind `CANARY_TELEMETRY_TAGS=1` or scope via service account.

Noah Patel: we can scope by `auth_scope=perf-canary` (see internal canary auth scope PR) and only emit the extra tags when that scope is present.

Vanessa Ortiz: also add to rollout checklist: confirm kernel tags visible in dashboard before enabling paging.

Hiro Tanaka: sounds good. can someone own the runtime PR to export tags?

Omar El-Sayed: i’ll take. will target adding:
- `runtime_release` (already there)
- `runtime_build_id` (stable build number)
- `kernel_bundle_id`
- `kernel_impl_group`
- `kernel_selector_version`
traces get all 5; metrics get first 4.

Logan Wright: can you drop `runtime_build_id` from metrics if it’s basically unique per deploy? maybe keep it in traces only.

Omar El-Sayed: ok. metrics: `runtime_release`, `kernel_bundle_id`, `kernel_impl_group` only.

Nadia Rahman: pls document the allowed values for `kernel_impl_group` so baselines don’t get junk.

Caleb Johnson: i can add a schema check in telemetry pipeline / observability pack to reject unknown values (or map to `unknown`).

Noah Patel: timeline: we want this before phase-1 paging enable. ideally by end of week.

Omar El-Sayed: acknowledged. i’ll post PR link here once up.

Hiro Tanaka: :thumbsup: thank you. this will save a lot of “is it kernels?” pages.

Logan Wright: final ask: add unit test that ensures tags are absent when not canary scope.

Omar El-Sayed: will do.

Sofia Ivanova: once this lands i’ll update the triage section in runbook: “check if kernel_impl_group changed vs baseline”.

Noah Patel: perfect. closing loop when PR is up.
