Sofia Ivanova: what about selection? i want to know if scheduler changes caused different batch shapes which caused selector to pick a different kernel. if we have `selector_ver` + `impl`, we can at least see “impl switched”.

Noah Patel: can we also log when impl switches for the same model/version/sku? like a counter: `kernel_selection_change_total`?

Omar El-Sayed: careful: “same shape” definition is tricky. but we can do a coarse counter in canary itself: during a run, track which impl ids observed per model and include it in the summary.

Caleb Johnson: metrics proposal (names tbd):
- `inference_runtime_request_latency_ms` (hist)
labels: `model`, `model_version`, `gpu_sku`, `region`, `runtime_release`, `kernel_bundle_id`, `kernel_impl_group`
where `kernel_impl_group` is a controlled enum like:
- `flashattn_v2`
- `flashattn_v3`
- `triton_sdpa`
- `cutlass_sdpa`
- `fallback_eager`
- `unknown`

Logan Wright: that label set might be ok if `kernel_impl_group` stays tiny. please do not add raw kernel name (`flash_v3_sm90_s128_kv8...`). also: do we really need `model_version` on *all* runtime metrics? we already agreed in cardinality review to prefer canonical model id + version only on canary-emitted metrics, not all prod traffic.

Omar El-Sayed: this is for canary traffic only? (perf-canary service account)

Noah Patel: yes, canary traffic + maybe a small sample on internal benchmark jobs. not general customer traffic.

Logan Wright: perfect. then cardinality is bounded by config (top models, a few regions). i’m good with `model_version` there.

Hiro Tanaka: can we also ensure alert payload includes these fields so we don’t have to click 3 dashboards?

Noah Patel: yep. incident-bot handler can show:
- model, region, sku
- runtime_release (observed)
- kernel_bundle_id + kernel_impl_group (mode over last N samples)
- baseline runtime_release + baseline kernel_bundle_id (if different)

Nadia Rahman: baseline store currently stores `runtime_release` and `gpu_sku` already. we can add optional fields `kernel_bundle_id` and `kernel_impl_group_mode`. as “metadata” only.

Sofia Ivanova: question: if kernel impl differs between baseline and observed, do we treat as regression automatically?

Hiro Tanaka: not automatically. but it’s a “big hint”. if selector changed, we should know immediately.

Omar El-Sayed: implementation detail: kernel selection happens in the attention op. we can plumb a thread-local “kernel id” that gets attached to the request context at end of request.

Caleb Johnson: if it’s thread-local, we need to be careful under async + batching. better: attach to request struct / trace context explicitly.

Omar El-Sayed: good point. in batching we process multiple requests in one kernel call, but can still attribute the impl per batch. probably record kernel tags per-batch and attach to each request span.

Logan Wright: for metrics, per-request labels are ok in canary. but please ensure we don’t accidentally enable this for all traffic later. guard behind `CANARY_TELEMETRY_TAGS=1` or scope via service account.

Noah Patel: we can scope by `auth_scope=perf-canary` (see internal canary auth scope PR) and only emit the extra tags when that scope is present.

Vanessa Ortiz: also add to rollout checklist: confirm kernel tags visible in dashboard before enabling paging.

Hiro Tanaka: sounds good. can someone own the runtime PR to export tags?

Omar El-Sayed: i’ll take. will target adding:
- `runtime_release` (already there)
- `runtime_build_id` (stable build number)
- `kernel_bundle_id`
- `kernel_impl_group`
- `kernel_selector_version`
traces get all 5; metrics get first 4.

Logan Wright: can you drop `runtime_build_id` from metrics if it’s basically unique per deploy? maybe keep it in traces only.

Omar El-Sayed: ok. metrics: `runtime_release`, `kernel_bundle_id`, `kernel_impl_group` only.

Nadia Rahman: pls document the allowed values for `kernel_impl_group` so baselines don’t get junk.

Caleb Johnson: i can add a schema check in telemetry pipeline / observability pack to reject unknown values (or map to `unknown`).

Noah Patel: timeline: we want this before phase-1 paging enable. ideally by end of week.

Omar El-Sayed: acknowledged. i’ll post PR link here once up.

Hiro Tanaka: :thumbsup: thank you. this will save a lot of “is it kernels?” pages.

Logan Wright: final ask: add unit test that ensures tags are absent when not canary scope.

Omar El-Sayed: will do.

Sofia Ivanova: once this lands i’ll update the triage section in runbook: “check if kernel_impl_group changed vs baseline”.

Noah Patel: perfect. closing loop when PR is up.

title: #eng-runtime: 1768604412
preview:
Nadia Rahman: flagging an issue we’re gonna hit w/ perf-canary baselines: baseline drift over weeks as fleet mix / traffic mix changes slowly. if we keep comparing to a fixed baseline, we’ll eventually trigger “regressions” even though nothing broke. conversely if baseline tracks too fast, we can mask real regressions. thoughts on the right strategy?

Noah Patel: +1. we already saw it in staging when we started running more long-context shapes at night. p95 “regressed” vs baseline but it was just prompt bucket mix shifting.

Sofia Ivanova: drift sources I’m seeing:
- hardware mix: A100 pool gradually replaced w/ H100 in us-east
- scheduler behavior: batch size distribution shifts w/ QPS
- workload shape: prompt buckets shift w/ product traffic + time of day
- kernel auto-selection: minor change in seq-len distribution changes chosen kernel

Logan Wright: from telemetry side, the biggest thing is to keep baseline dimensions stable. if we store baseline at (model, runtime_build, gpu_sku, region) we reduce drift a lot. BUT if gpu_sku is missing/incorrect or we include too many labels, we’ll get cardinality pain.

Allison Grant: drift is inevitable even w/ good dimensions. question is: do we want baseline to be:
A) “golden” per runtime version (fixed after calibration) => good for regression detection, but noisy during gradual infra changes
B) rolling baseline (EWMA / rolling window) => stable alerting, but can chase the metric and hide slow regressions
Maybe we do both: fixed baseline for gating releases, rolling baseline for day-to-day paging?

Nadia Rahman: yeah, I was thinking dual-baseline:
- release-gate baseline: pinned for runtime_build+kernel_tag+sku, computed from controlled run
- ops baseline: rolling, for catching sudden deltas, but only used for warning-level maybe

Rafael Mendes: from oncall POV: anything that pages needs low noise. if “baseline drift” can page us at 3am, we’ll mute it within a week :grimacing:

Aisha Bello: agree. we can still surface drift as a dashboard “baseline aging” or “baseline mismatch” indicator, but paging should be about step changes.

Vanessa Ortiz: infra drift is real. us-east: we’ve been rebalancing dedicated pools and moving some customers to new nodes w/ different driver versions. metrics will move even if runtime is unchanged.

Omar El-Sayed: can we detect infra drift explicitly? e.g. track driver version / cuda / node image as tags and check if canary is running on mixed versions.

Logan Wright: we can tag “fleet_build_id” / “node_image” but need to be careful. that’s high-cardinality if it’s per node. better is a coarse “fleet_generation” or “driver_branch” label.

Sofia Ivanova: +1. also for drift due to workload mix, canary is synthetic so *we control* that. the only mix drift should be our own config change. which is good: we can version the workload profile.

Noah Patel: right. for canary runs, we should pin the shape distribution + decoding params. if we need to change them, treat it like a new baseline version.

Nadia Rahman: ok so workload drift is solvable by versioning `workload_profile_id` and making baseline key include it.

Allison Grant: baseline key proposal (minimal):
- model_id (canonical)
- model_version (registry)
- runtime_build_id
- kernel_selection_id (or kernel_family)
- gpu_sku
- region
- workload_profile_id
Anything else is “extra tags” for debugging but not baseline indexing.

Logan Wright: that’s already borderline, but manageable if model list is small (top N). we should ensure kernel_selection_id is low-cardinality (not per-request). e.g. “flashattn_v2” not “kernel_hash_abc123”.

Noah Patel: on baseline update policy: can we do “rolling” but with a cap on slope? like EWMA with a long half-life, plus a “step change” detector that pages if abrupt delta.

Nadia Rahman: I sketched something like this:

```
# observed metric over window W: x_t
# baseline mean: b_t
# update only if no active regression + enough samples

alpha = 0.02  # ~50-sample half-life-ish
b_{t+1} = (1 - alpha) * b_t + alpha * x_t

# regression score uses b_t (pre-update)
regress = (x_t - b_t) / b_t

# guardrails:
# - if |regress| > warn_threshold: DO NOT update baseline
# - if sample_count < min_samples: DO NOT update baseline
# - clamp baseline update to +/- max_daily_drift
```

But I’m worried alpha tuning becomes a whole thing.

Aisha Bello: I like guardrails: “don’t learn during an incident”. otherwise the baseline will self-heal and we’ll lose history.

Rafael Mendes: also add anti-flap: require N consecutive windows over threshold before page.

Sofia Ivanova: one more thought: drift from *fleet mix* doesn’t affect canary if canary is pinned to a specific pool/SKU. but canary will run on whatever the target model is running on. if that pool is gradually changing under the hood, that’s drift.

Vanessa Ortiz: right, dedicated pools especially: we’ll roll node image in waves. during the wave, performance moves ~2–4% for some models.

Allison Grant: so maybe incorporate a “known-change” suppressor. if fleet change in progress for that region/SKU, downgrade to warning or extend dampening.

Logan Wright: we can wire that via an annotation stream from infra (“fleet_event: driver_rollout”) and have alert rules include it. but that’s extra integration.

Noah Patel: we can do a simpler v1: baseline includes gpu_sku only. if drift happens due to driver rollout, we’ll see it but can attribute via tags in the alert payload (driver_branch). oncall can then decide whether to mute.

Rafael Mendes: ok but we need a documented “mute policy” and ideally a bot command. otherwise we’ll get random manual silences.

Nadia Rahman: action items I’m hearing:
1) baseline keys include workload_profile_id and gpu_sku + region + runtime_build_id (and kernel_family)
2) baseline is fixed per runtime release gate (computed from controlled run)
3) alerting compares observed vs fixed baseline; for drift handling we add either:
   a) rolling “ops baseline” shown in dashboards only (not paging)
   b) optional slow EWMA update BUT frozen when in warning/page state
4) add tags in payload to help explain drift: driver_branch, fleet_generation (coarse)

Allison Grant: +1. also add “baseline freshness” signal: if baseline is >X days old AND we’ve had sustained deltas < threshold, surface “needs recalibration” instead of “regression”.

Aisha Bello: baseline freshness panel + a non-paging ticket alert (“recalibrate baseline for model X / region Y”) would be perfect.

Logan Wright: can do. we can emit a gauge `perf_canary_baseline_age_hours` and a boolean `perf_canary_baseline_stale` based on last_updated.

Sofia Ivanova: for the “slow drift” case: if we see monotonic movement 0.5–1% per day, that’s not a page, but it’s worth tracking. maybe create a weekly report?

Noah Patel: yeah. page is for step regressions, warn is for moderate sustained, report/ticket is for slow drift.

Vanessa Ortiz: if we’re going to attribute infra drift, pls include in runbook: check fleet status page + recent driver rollouts.

Rafael Mendes: I’ll add that to the runbook draft once we settle the baseline logic.

Nadia Rahman: cool. I’ll update the ADR draft w/ “fixed baseline for regression detection + baseline freshness + optional rolling for dashboards” and propose stale->ticket not page. will tag folks for review.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: tag me + Aisha for alerting semantics.

Logan Wright: and me for label/cardinality sanity check.

Noah Patel: same. also, if we include kernel_family in baseline, we need agreement on naming. I’ll sync w/ Hiro/Omar.

Omar El-Sayed: I’ll propose a small enum list for kernel_family and make sure it’s coming through telemetry consistently.

Nadia Rahman: awesome. thanks all.

title: #eng-runtime: 1768512097
preview:
Sofia Ivanova: pulling in folks from cost-model thread. question from @Ethan Park/@Logan Wright: which batching metrics can we commit to as *stable* for v1 cost-model contract? we have like 8 different "batch size" numbers floating around :grimacing:

Ethan Park: yep. we need something that won’t churn every runtime release. goal is to model batch efficiency + queue time in cost breakdown. what can we reliably export per-request and/or per-batch?

Logan Wright: +1. telemetry wants a few canonical fields with clear semantics + units. otherwise we’re stuck with free-text tags.

Omar El-Sayed: ok, current state:
- scheduler builds a "batch" object, but it can change between prefill and decode
- continuous batching means a request can join a decode wave after initial prefill
- we also have chunking on long prompts (prefill can be split)
so if you ask for "batch_size" without specifying stage, it’s ambiguous.

Grace Kim: also some counters are from kernel path (effective tokens) vs scheduler (num sequences). we should not expose kernel-internal "pack ratio" / "tile occupancy" etc. those change per arch.

Thomas Baird: cost-model needs something that correlates with throughput. if we can’t give true occupancy, a proxy like "num_active_seqs" per decode step is fine, as long as it’s consistent.

Sofia Ivanova: proposal: define stable metrics at two levels:
1) per-request attribution fields (safe for high cardinality)
2) per-batch aggregate fields (safe, but need bounding)

Ethan Park: we prefer per-request fields that can be aggregated upstream. console hot path can’t pull per-batch for every batch id anyway.

Nadia Rahman: +1. also token attribution is per-request; batch ids are annoying because retries/fallbacks can break joins.

Omar El-Sayed: ok, what we can commit to for v1 (stable + already close to existing instrumentation):
Per request (for each runtime "attempt" / model execution):
- `batching.prefill_cohort_size` = number of requests that shared the *final* prefill launch with this request (int)
- `batching.decode_cohort_avg_size` = avg number of active sequences in the decode wave(s) while this request was decoding (float; weighted by decode tokens)
- `batching.decode_cohort_p95_size` = p95 active sequences during decode steps for this request (int)
- `scheduler.queue_time_ms` = time from admission to first prefill launch (ms)
- `scheduler.decode_queue_time_ms` = total time spent waiting between decode steps due to scheduling (ms)
Notes:
- cohort sizes are defined at scheduler layer, not kernel
- all measured for a specific `attempt_id`

Grace Kim: I like those because they’re stage-scoped. two gotchas:
1) chunked prefill: prompt split into segments. do we define prefill cohort size per segment? 
2) decode cohort: active sequences can vary per step; avg/p95 is ok.

Sofia Ivanova: for (1) we should define "final prefill launch" = the last prefill segment launch (the one after which decode begins). that’s usually the segment that matters for latency + batch contention.

Omar El-Sayed: yep. alternatively we can export `prefill_segments` count later as v2, but I’d rather not block v1.

Logan Wright: can we also export `batching.prefill_total_tokens_in_cohort`? token-weighted measure could be better than just cohort size.

Grace Kim: careful. that requires summing across requests in same batch and emitting it to each request (fanout). expensive and risks bugs.

Thomas Baird: also cardinality/duplication. if we emit cohort totals per request we amplify data volume.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. I’d keep it to cohort sizes + times. cost model can approximate batch efficiency using cohort size + known model/hw throughput curves.

Ethan Park: makes sense. cost-model v1 can compute "batch_efficiency_factor" from:
- prefill_cohort_size
- decode_cohort_avg_size
- sequence length distribution (from token metering)
- runtime profile (from model catalog)

Nadia Rahman: sanity check: what about dynamic batching window? do we expose `batching.window_ms`?

Sofia Ivanova: that’s config, not observed metric. and it changes per deployment. better as an input (deployment config) from control plane, not runtime telemetry.

Omar El-Sayed: agree. runtime can export observed queue time; control plane can provide configured window/limits.

Logan Wright: cool. what do we say is *not stable* / not in contract?

Grace Kim: list of "do not depend":
- internal scheduler "pack score" (heuristic)
- kernel occupancy / warp stats
- microbatch id / internal batch UUID (changes on retries)
- per-step decode batch size time series (too heavy)
- "effective_batch_size" currently computed in a debug-only way

Sofia Ivanova: also `scheduler.batch_reason` enums are not stable (we keep changing names). if you need it later, we’ll version it.

Thomas Baird: do we need a single canonical name for cohort size? I’d avoid `batch_size` entirely in schema.

Ethan Park: +100. we’ll use the explicit stage-scoped names Omar listed.

Omar El-Sayed: one more: we can add `scheduler.max_concurrent_seqs_limit` (configured limit observed by runtime). but that’s basically config again.

Ethan Park: let’s defer to v2. for v1 we’ll treat max batch limits as control plane/model profile inputs.

Logan Wright: action items?

Sofia Ivanova: - I’ll post the proposed field defs to the schema PR thread (ENG-4822).
- @Omar El-Sayed can you confirm you can export the 5 fields above off the runtime metrics stream without extra joins?

Omar El-Sayed: yes. queue times are already there; cohort sizes need a small change in scheduler metrics emitter but it’s local.

Grace Kim: please make sure `decode_cohort_avg_size` is token-weighted, not step-weighted, otherwise long decode steps skew it.

Omar El-Sayed: ack. token-weighted by decode tokens per step (we already compute per-step decode tokens for accounting).

Nadia Rahman: and define units + rounding rules. floats ok, but we should specify precision (e.g., 2 decimals) to keep payload stable.

Ethan Park: good call. we’ll specify:
- avg_size: float64, recommended emit with 2 decimal places
- times: int64 ms

Thomas Baird: (reaction: :thumbsup:)

Sofia Ivanova: last thing: does `decode_queue_time_ms` include time when request is finished but batch continues? should be only while request is active.

Omar El-Sayed: only while request is active. definition: sum over intervals where request has remaining decode tokens and is not scheduled to run.

Grace Kim: perfect. that’s the thing cost model cares about.

Logan Wright: thanks all. I’ll update telemetry contract doc to call these out as runtime-sourced fields.

Ethan Park: awesome. once these are in, cost model can stop guessing from batch_efficiency debug metric.

Sofia Ivanova: closing the loop here after I paste defs into schema PR. ping if anyone objects in the next day or two.

- `batching.prefill_cohort_size` = number of requests that shared the *final* prefill launch with this request (int)
- `batching.decode_cohort_avg_size` = avg number of active sequences in the decode wave(s) while this request was decoding (float; weighted by decode tokens)
- `batching.decode_cohort_p95_size` = p95 active sequences during decode steps for this request (int)
- `scheduler.queue_time_ms` = time from admission to first prefill launch (ms)
- `scheduler.decode_queue_time_ms` = total time spent waiting between decode steps due to scheduling (ms)
Notes:
- cohort sizes are defined at scheduler layer, not kernel
- all measured for a specific `attempt_id`

Grace Kim: I like those because they’re stage-scoped. two gotchas:
1) chunked prefill: prompt split into segments. do we define prefill cohort size per segment? 
2) decode cohort: active sequences can vary per step; avg/p95 is ok.

Sofia Ivanova: for (1) we should define "final prefill launch" = the last prefill segment launch (the one after which decode begins). that’s usually the segment that matters for latency + batch contention.

Omar El-Sayed: yep. alternatively we can export `prefill_segments` count later as v2, but I’d rather not block v1.

Logan Wright: can we also export `batching.prefill_total_tokens_in_cohort`? token-weighted measure could be better than just cohort size.

Grace Kim: careful. that requires summing across requests in same batch and emitting it to each request (fanout). expensive and risks bugs.

Thomas Baird: also cardinality/duplication. if we emit cohort totals per request we amplify data volume.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. I’d keep it to cohort sizes + times. cost model can approximate batch efficiency using cohort size + known model/hw throughput curves.

Ethan Park: makes sense. cost-model v1 can compute "batch_efficiency_factor" from:
- prefill_cohort_size
- decode_cohort_avg_size
- sequence length distribution (from token metering)
- runtime profile (from model catalog)

Nadia Rahman: sanity check: what about dynamic batching window? do we expose `batching.window_ms`?

Sofia Ivanova: that’s config, not observed metric. and it changes per deployment. better as an input (deployment config) from control plane, not runtime telemetry.

Omar El-Sayed: agree. runtime can export observed queue time; control plane can provide configured window/limits.

Logan Wright: cool. what do we say is *not stable* / not in contract?

Grace Kim: list of "do not depend":
- internal scheduler "pack score" (heuristic)
- kernel occupancy / warp stats
- microbatch id / internal batch UUID (changes on retries)
- per-step decode batch size time series (too heavy)
- "effective_batch_size" currently computed in a debug-only way

Sofia Ivanova: also `scheduler.batch_reason` enums are not stable (we keep changing names). if you need it later, we’ll version it.

Thomas Baird: do we need a single canonical name for cohort size? I’d avoid `batch_size` entirely in schema.

Ethan Park: +100. we’ll use the explicit stage-scoped names Omar listed.

Omar El-Sayed: one more: we can add `scheduler.max_concurrent_seqs_limit` (configured limit observed by runtime). but that’s basically config again.

Ethan Park: let’s defer to v2. for v1 we’ll treat max batch limits as control plane/model profile inputs.

Logan Wright: action items?

Sofia Ivanova: - I’ll post the proposed field defs to the schema PR thread (ENG-4822).
- @Omar El-Sayed can you confirm you can export the 5 fields above off the runtime metrics stream without extra joins?

Omar El-Sayed: yes. queue times are already there; cohort sizes need a small change in scheduler metrics emitter but it’s local.

Grace Kim: please make sure `decode_cohort_avg_size` is token-weighted, not step-weighted, otherwise long decode steps skew it.

Omar El-Sayed: ack. token-weighted by decode tokens per step (we already compute per-step decode tokens for accounting).

Nadia Rahman: and define units + rounding rules. floats ok, but we should specify precision (e.g., 2 decimals) to keep payload stable.

Ethan Park: good call. we’ll specify:
- avg_size: float64, recommended emit with 2 decimal places
- times: int64 ms

Thomas Baird: (reaction: :thumbsup:)

Sofia Ivanova: last thing: does `decode_queue_time_ms` include time when request is finished but batch continues? should be only while request is active.

Omar El-Sayed: only while request is active. definition: sum over intervals where request has remaining decode tokens and is not scheduled to run.

Grace Kim: perfect. that’s the thing cost model cares about.

Logan Wright: thanks all. I’ll update telemetry contract doc to call these out as runtime-sourced fields.

Ethan Park: awesome. once these are in, cost model can stop guessing from batch_efficiency debug metric.

Sofia Ivanova: closing the loop here after I paste defs into schema PR. ping if anyone objects in the next day or two.
